muscle_fiber_types.q: What is the contractile unit of skeletal muscle, and how is it bounded?
muscle_fiber_types.a: The sarcomere — the region from one Z-line to the next (~2.5 µm in a relaxed fiber).

muscle_fiber_types.q: Order the structural hierarchy from whole muscle down to the contractile unit.
muscle_fiber_types.a: Muscle → fasciculus → muscle fiber (cell) → myofibril → sarcomere.

muscle_fiber_types.q: Which fiber type has the largest diameter and highest force/power but fatigues fastest?
In the cross-bridge cycle, which step specifically triggers the power stroke?
muscle_fiber_types.a: Release of inorganic phosphate (Pi) from the myosin head after it binds actin — this pivots the head, pulling actin toward the sarcomere center.

muscle_fiber_types.q: What role does ATP play in detachment, and what happens without it?
muscle_fiber_types.a: A new ATP binding the myosin head dissociates the cross-bridge and re-cocks it. Without ATP the head stays bound — the rigor state.

muscle_fiber_types.q: Why do Type I fibers resist fatigue better than Type II?
muscle_fiber_types.a: Higher mitochondrial density/distribution, more capillaries and myoglobin, and higher aerobic-enzyme content → greater ATP production via aerobic metabolism.

muscle_fiber_types.q: Name the two ways a muscle grades its force output.
muscle_fiber_types.a: Recruiting more motor units (size principle) and increasing motor-unit firing frequency (rate coding → twitch summation → tetanus).

muscle_fiber_types.q: Structurally, what changes inside the sarcomere during contraction?
muscle_fiber_types.a: Actin slides inward over myosin; Z-lines are pulled toward center; the H-zone and I-band shrink (A-band width unchanged).

muscle_fiber_types.q: A runner claims training 'converted' their Type IIx fibers to Type I. What's wrong with that?
muscle_fiber_types.a: Training shifts IIx → IIa within the fast pool; it doesn't convert Type II to Type I. Slow/fast proportion is largely genetic.

muscle_fiber_types.q: Per the size principle, which motor units fire during an easy jog vs. a 1RM deadlift?
muscle_fiber_types.a: Easy jog: low-threshold slow (Type I) units. 1RM deadlift: near-full recruitment adding high-threshold fast (IIa/IIx) units.

muscle_fiber_types.q: Which fiber type dominates a marathon's later miles vs. an Olympic-lift attempt?
muscle_fiber_types.a: Late marathon miles: Type I (oxidative, fatigue-resistant). Olympic lift: high recruitment with major Type II (IIa/IIx) contribution.

muscle_fiber_types.q: For a Hyrox athlete, why does developing Type IIa matter more than chasing pure IIx?
muscle_fiber_types.a: Stations demand repeated near-maximal efforts under fatigue. IIa gives fast force with better fatigue resistance than IIx, so threshold/tempo work that builds fatigue-resistant fast fibers pays off across 8 stations.

muscle_fiber_types.q: Force is directly proportional to what, at the filament level?
muscle_fiber_types.a: The number of actin–myosin cross-bridges formed within the muscle's fibers.

motor_units.a: One motor neuron plus all the muscle fibers it innervates; every fiber in the unit is the same type and contracts together.
Motor units are recruited from smallest/lowest-threshold (Type I) to largest/highest-threshold (Type IIa then IIx) as force demand increases.

motor_units.q: What two mechanisms grade muscle force?
motor_units.a: Recruitment (number of active motor units) and rate coding (firing frequency of the motor neurons).

motor_units.q: What does the all-or-none principle mean for a motor unit?
motor_units.a: When its motor neuron fires, all fibers in the unit contract; there is no partial activation of a single unit.

motor_units.q: Why do small muscles (eye, hand) rely mainly on rate coding rather than recruitment?
motor_units.a: Most of their motor units are already active even at low forces, so force is increased chiefly by raising firing frequency.

motor_units.q: In the weighted vs unweighted arm-curl example, what differs in recruitment?
motor_units.a: The unweighted curl recruits only low-threshold slow-twitch units; the weighted curl adds higher-threshold fast-twitch units — the size principle in action.

motor_units.q: Roughly what fraction of available motor units does maximal high-intensity weightlifting activate (EMG estimate)?
motor_units.a: About 90% or more of available motor units in the involved muscles.

motor_units.q: Why can't Zone-2 cycling develop your Type IIx units?
motor_units.a: Its force demand stays below the recruitment threshold of high-threshold units, so only low-threshold Type I units are engaged.

motor_units.q: How does a twitch become tetanus?
motor_units.a: Increasing firing frequency makes successive twitches summate/overlap into unfused tetanus, then fused (smooth) tetanus = the unit's maximal force.

motor_units.q: Rank Type I, IIa, IIx by recruitment threshold and force output.
motor_units.a: Threshold and force/RFD both rise I → IIa → IIx; IIx has the highest threshold and greatest force but least fatigue resistance.

motor_units.q: A power clean requires near-maximal force in a very short time. What does this demand of motor unit activation?
motor_units.a: Recruitment of most available units, with fast-twitch units contributing significantly to the high, rapid force output.

motor_units.q: Why might an untrained athlete fail to hit true maximal force even at all-out effort?
motor_units.a: Full activation of the motor-neuron pool is likely not possible when untrained, and high-threshold units may not be driven at a high enough firing frequency.

ec_coupling.q: Name the neurotransmitter at the neuromuscular junction and where it binds.
ec_coupling.a: Acetylcholine (ACh); it binds ACh receptors on the sarcolemma, generating an action potential along the muscle fiber.

ec_coupling.q: Trace the path of the signal from sarcolemmal AP to Ca²⁺ release.
ec_coupling.a: Sarcolemmal AP → propagates down T-tubules → triggers the sarcoplasmic reticulum to release stored Ca²⁺ into the myofibrils.

ec_coupling.q: What does Ca²⁺ actually bind, and what does that binding accomplish?
ec_coupling.a: Ca²⁺ binds troponin, which shifts tropomyosin off actin, exposing the myosin-binding sites so crossbridges can form.

ec_coupling.q: According to sliding-filament theory, which sarcomere regions shorten during contraction and which stays the same?
ec_coupling.a: The H-zone and I-band shorten as actin slides over myosin; the A-band (myosin length) stays constant.

ec_coupling.q: List the crossbridge cycle steps in order.
ec_coupling.a: 1) Myosin hydrolyzes ATP → cocked high-energy head; 2) binds actin; 3) Pi release → power stroke pulls actin inward; 4) ADP released; 5) new ATP binds → head detaches and re-cocks.

ec_coupling.q: Where in the crossbridge cycle is ATP consumed, and why does detachment need a fresh molecule?
ec_coupling.a: ATP is hydrolyzed to cock the head (energizing the power stroke), and a new ATP must bind for the head to detach from actin. Two distinct ATP roles.
At death ATP production stops; without fresh ATP to bind, myosin cannot detach from actin, so crossbridges stay locked and the muscle stiffens.

ec_coupling.q: How is the muscle returned to a relaxed state after the stimulus ends?
ec_coupling.a: Motor-neuron firing stops; ATP-dependent pumps (SERCA) return Ca²⁺ to the SR, lowering myofibril Ca²⁺, so tropomyosin re-covers actin and crossbridges dissociate.

ec_coupling.q: What determines how much force a muscle produces at a given instant?
ec_coupling.a: The number of actin-myosin crossbridges bound at that instant. High force demands recruiting most motor units to maximize crossbridge formation.

ec_coupling.q: State the all-or-none principle for a motor unit.
ec_coupling.a: Activating a given motor neuron leads to the activation and contraction of all muscle fibers it innervates. (The 5E notes it is used as a general description, since evidence suggests individual fibers can be partially activated within an active unit.)

ec_coupling.q: A single twitch produces low force despite releasing enough Ca²⁺ for crossbridging — why, and how is high force achieved?
ec_coupling.a: Ca²⁺ is re-sequestered within milliseconds so the fiber relaxes before force peaks. Higher firing frequency summates twitches → unfused then fused tetanus, giving maximal force.

ec_coupling.q: Which two ions/molecules are both required for crossbridge cycling, and what is each one's role?
ec_coupling.a: Ca²⁺ (uncovers actin binding sites via troponin) and ATP (cocks the myosin head for the power stroke and enables detachment).

cardiovascular.q: Write the cardiac output equation and give typical rest and max values.
cardiovascular.a: Q = SV × HR. Rest ~5 L/min; max ~20-22 L/min (about 4× rest).

cardiovascular.q: At what fraction of VO₂max does stroke volume plateau?
cardiovascular.a: Around 40-50% of VO₂max; beyond that, further rises in Q come mainly from increasing HR.

cardiovascular.q: State the Frank-Starling mechanism and the vessel changes that feed it during exercise.
cardiovascular.a: Increased end-diastolic filling stretches myocardial fibers → more forceful contraction → ↑SV. Venous return rises via venoconstriction, the skeletal-muscle pump, and the respiratory pump, raising EDV.

cardiovascular.q: Order the cardiac conduction pathway and give the SA node's intrinsic rate.
cardiovascular.a: SA node → internodal pathways → AV node (brief delay) → bundle branches → Purkinje fibers. SA node fires ~60-80/min, making it the dominant pacemaker.

cardiovascular.q: Why does the AV node delay the impulse?
cardiovascular.a: To let the atria finish contracting and fill the ventricles before ventricular contraction begins.

cardiovascular.q: Trace blood through the vessel types from heart to heart and give each one's job.
cardiovascular.a: Arteries (transport under high pressure) → arterioles (resistance/flow control) → capillaries (exchange) → venules → veins (low-pressure return, blood reservoir with one-way valves).

cardiovascular.q: Which vessel type holds the largest share of blood volume at rest, and roughly how much?
cardiovascular.a: Veins, venules, and venous sinuses — about 64% of total blood volume.

cardiovascular.q: Estimate HRmax for a 47-year-old two ways.
Using the Fick equation, compute VO₂ for HR 72, SV 65 mL, a-vO₂ difference 6 mL O₂/100 mL.
cardiovascular.a: VO₂ = HR × SV × a-vO₂ diff = 72 × 65 × 6/100 = 281 mL O₂/min. For an 80 kg athlete: 281 ÷ 80 ≈ 3.5 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ (≈1 MET).

cardiovascular.q: Compute mean arterial pressure for a 120/80 reading.
cardiovascular.a: MAP = [(SBP − DBP)/3] + DBP = (40/3) + 80 ≈ 93 mmHg. It sits nearer diastolic because the heart spends more of the cycle in diastole.

cardiovascular.q: An athlete's RHR drops 65→50 bpm over 6 months with no loss of capacity. Mechanism?
cardiovascular.a: Increased SV from cardiac adaptation (larger, better-filling LV) means a lower HR sustains the same resting Q; enhanced parasympathetic tone also contributes.

cardiovascular.q: During a hard run SBP climbs to 240 mmHg but DBP stays ~80. Is that expected? Why?
cardiovascular.a: Yes. Systolic can normally rise to 220-260 mmHg with maximal aerobic work, while diastolic holds or dips slightly because of peripheral vasodilation reducing resistance.

cardiovascular.q: Two athletes have the same VO₂max but one has higher SV. What differs about their HR at a fixed submax Q?
cardiovascular.a: The higher-SV athlete needs a lower HR to deliver the same Q, giving more filling time and greater cardiac reserve — a hallmark of endurance conditioning.

cardiovascular.q: Why does maintaining leg-muscle contraction (or cadence) matter for stroke volume during prolonged work?
cardiovascular.a: The skeletal-muscle pump compresses veins and, with one-way valves, drives venous return — sustaining EDV and SV via Frank-Starling; stopping lets blood pool and SV fall.

respiratory.q: What is the Bohr effect and why does it matter during exercise?
respiratory.a: A rightward shift of the O₂-Hb curve caused by ↑CO₂, ↑temp, or ↓pH; it lowers Hb's O₂ affinity so more O₂ is released to active muscle exactly where metabolic demand is highest.

respiratory.q: About how many airway generations does air pass before reaching the alveoli?
respiratory.a: Approximately 23 generations (trachea = 1st, main bronchi = 2nd, then successive bronchiole divisions).

respiratory.q: At low-to-moderate intensity, is the rise in ventilation driven mainly by tidal volume or frequency?
respiratory.a: Mainly tidal volume (deeper breathing); breathing frequency takes over only at higher intensities as TV plateaus.

respiratory.q: Why is increasing tidal volume more efficient than increasing breathing frequency?
respiratory.a: Anatomical dead space (~150 mL) increases proportionally less than tidal volume, so deeper breaths deliver a larger fraction of air to gas-exchanging alveoli.

respiratory.q: Calculate minute ventilation for an athlete breathing 40 br/min at a tidal volume of 3.0 L.
How is CO₂ transported in the blood, and in what proportions?
respiratory.a: The greatest share (~70%) as bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻), only about 5% dissolved in plasma, and the rest bound to hemoglobin (a limited amount); carbonic anhydrase in the red blood cells speeds bicarbonate formation.

respiratory.q: What is the ventilatory equivalent, and how does it change from rest to high-intensity exercise?
respiratory.a: V̇E/V̇O₂ — ratio of minute ventilation to O₂ uptake. ~20-25 L air per L O₂ at rest/moderate, rising to 35-40 at high intensity as ventilation outpaces O₂ uptake and parallels the lactate rise.

respiratory.q: In a healthy non-elite athlete, what typically limits VO₂max — the lungs, the heart, or the muscle?
respiratory.a: Central (cardiac output) and peripheral (mitochondrial/capillary) factors. The pulmonary system rarely limits VO₂max in healthy non-elites.

respiratory.q: At rest, roughly how much greater is alveolar PO₂ than pulmonary-capillary PO₂, and which way does O₂ diffuse?
respiratory.a: About 60 mmHg greater in the alveoli, so O₂ diffuses from alveoli into the pulmonary capillary blood.

respiratory.q: What happens to tissue PO₂ and PCO₂ going from rest to high-intensity exercise?
respiratory.a: Interstitial PO₂ falls from ~40 mmHg (rest) toward ~3 mmHg; PCO₂ rises from ~46 mmHg toward ~90 mmHg — steepening gradients that speed O₂ delivery and CO₂ removal.

respiratory.q: What fraction of total body energy expenditure goes to breathing at rest vs heavy exercise?
respiratory.a: ~3-5% at rest, rising to as much as 8-15% during heavy exercise (higher with airway resistance, e.g. exercise-induced asthma).

respiratory.q: Estimate the fold-increase in minute ventilation from rest to maximal exercise.
respiratory.a: About 15-25× resting values (e.g. ~6 L/min at rest → 90-150 L/min at max).

respiratory.q: How does the S-shape of the O₂-Hb dissociation curve benefit tissue O₂ delivery?
respiratory.a: On the steep portion, a small drop in tissue PO₂ releases a large amount of O₂, while the flat upper plateau keeps arterial Hb near-fully saturated (~98.5%) despite modest PO₂ variation.

atp_pcr.q: What reaction does creatine kinase catalyze, and why is it so fast?
atp_pcr.a: CP + ADP → ATP + Cr. It is a single-step reaction, so it regenerates ATP at a very high rate.

atp_pcr.q: Per Table 3.2, which energy system dominates an extremely-high-intensity 0-6 s effort?
How much CP is stored relative to ATP in resting muscle, and why does it matter?
atp_pcr.a: CP is 4-6x muscle ATP concentration, so it acts as a rapid ATP reserve that buffers ATP during explosive work.

atp_pcr.q: What is the adenylate (myokinase) reaction and why is its product important?
atp_pcr.a: 2 ADP → ATP + AMP. The AMP produced is a powerful stimulant of glycolysis, linking the phosphagen system to the next energy pathway.

atp_pcr.q: By roughly how much can muscle ATP fall during induced fatigue, and why not more?
atp_pcr.a: Only ~50-60% of pre-exercise levels; it cannot be fully depleted because ATP is required for basic cellular function.

atp_pcr.q: Explain how the law of mass action controls the phosphagen reactions during a set.
atp_pcr.a: As ATP hydrolyzes, ADP and Pi rise transiently, which speeds the creatine kinase and adenylate kinase reactions to rebuild ATP. When intensity drops and ATP rises, those reactions slow or reverse.

atp_pcr.q: You program 60 s rest between heavy back-squat singles. What is the bioenergetic problem?
atp_pcr.a: Full CP repletion needs ~3-5 min (up to ~8). At 60 s only about half of CP is back, so Type II force output falls on the next set. Extend rest to 3-5 min for maximal-strength work.

atp_pcr.q: Why does the phosphagen system have the highest rate but lowest capacity of ATP production?
atp_pcr.a: Its single-step reactions are extremely fast, but CP is stored in only small amounts, so total ATP it can supply is limited — an inverse rate-vs-capacity relationship (Table 3.3).

atp_pcr.q: How long until ~50% of CP is replenished, and what does this enable?
atp_pcr.a: About 30 seconds, which allows repeated near-maximal contractions interspersed with brief rest intervals.

atp_pcr.q: Why might an athlete with a higher percentage of Type II fibers replenish ATP faster via the phosphagen system?
atp_pcr.a: Type II fibers contain higher CP concentrations than Type I, giving a larger phosphate reserve for rapid ATP resynthesis in explosive work.

atp_pcr.q: During short-duration high-intensity exercise (5-30 s), what happens to CP vs ATP?
atp_pcr.a: CP decreases markedly and can be nearly fully depleted, while ATP falls only slightly to at most ~50-60%.

atp_pcr.q: Why is CP said to serve as an 'energy reserve' rather than the primary long-duration fuel?
atp_pcr.a: It rebuilds ATP fast to maintain ATP concentration, but it is stored in small amounts, so it cannot sustain continuous, long-duration activity.

glycolysis.q: What is the net ATP yield of glycolysis from glucose vs glycogen, and why the difference?
glycolysis.a: 2 ATP from glucose, 3 from glycogen. Glucose entry costs an ATP at hexokinase; glycogen is already phosphorylated (glycogen phosphorylase), so it skips that cost.

glycolysis.q: Gross ATP produced by substrate-level phosphorylation in glycolysis is 4. Why is the glucose net only 2?
glycolysis.a: Two ATP are consumed on the way in — one at hexokinase (glucose phosphorylation) and one at PFK. 4 - 2 = 2 net.

glycolysis.q: What actually determines whether pyruvate goes to lactate vs into the mitochondria?
glycolysis.a: Energy demand, not oxygen. High/fast demand → pyruvate to lactate (fast glycolysis); lower demand with adequate O2 → mitochondrial oxidation (slow glycolysis). Glycolysis itself is O2-independent.

glycolysis.q: True or false: lactate causes the acidosis and fatigue of hard exercise.
glycolysis.a: False. The H+ of metabolic acidosis comes mainly from ATP hydrolysis; the LDH reaction actually consumes protons. Lactate is a fuel/gluconeogenic substrate, not the cause of fatigue.

glycolysis.q: Define the lactate threshold (LT/LT1) and give typical intensities for untrained vs trained.
glycolysis.a: The intensity where blood lactate first climbs sharply above baseline, marking greater glycolytic reliance. ~50-60% VO2max untrained; 70-80% in trained endurance athletes.

glycolysis.q: What is OBLA, and at roughly what blood lactate concentration does it occur?
glycolysis.a: Onset of blood lactate accumulation (LT2) — the second inflection in the lactate curve, occurring at approximately 4 mmol/L.

glycolysis.q: Why do type II fibers produce more lactate than type I?
glycolysis.a: Higher glycolytic enzyme concentration/activity. Max lactate production ≈ 0.5 mmol/g/s in type II vs 0.25 in type I. Larger motor units (type II) recruited at higher intensities also coincide with LT/OBLA breakpoints.

glycolysis.q: An athlete's LT2 is 158 bpm; an interval set averages HR 172. What's happening metabolically?
glycolysis.a: Above MLSS/OBLA — fast glycolysis contributes heavily and lactate accumulates faster than it clears. The effort is supra-threshold and inherently time-limited.

glycolysis.q: How does the Cori cycle recycle lactate?
glycolysis.a: Muscle lactate travels to the liver, is converted to glucose via gluconeogenesis, and returns to the blood/muscle as fuel — sparing carbohydrate during extended exercise and recovery.

glycolysis.q: When does peak blood lactate occur after a maximal effort, and why the delay?
glycolysis.a: About 5 minutes post-exercise — the lag reflects time to buffer and transport lactate from muscle into the blood.

glycolysis.q: What is the net reaction of glycolysis to lactate (from glucose)?
glycolysis.a: Glucose + 2 Pi + 2 ADP → 2 lactate + 2 ATP + 2 H2O.

glycolysis.q: Approximately how much ATP does complete oxidation of one glucose (glycolysis + Krebs + ETC) yield, and how much from glycogen?
glycolysis.a: ~32 ATP from blood glucose; ~34 from muscle glycogen (malate-aspartate shuttle), since glycogen skips the hexokinase ATP cost. Values vary (~30-36) with the NADH shuttle used.

glycolysis.q: Practically, how does an athlete shift LT/OBLA to the right, and why does that matter?
glycolysis.a: Train at or near LT/OBLA (and above). It lets lactate accumulation occur later at a higher intensity, so the athlete sustains a faster pace before threshold — directly improving endurance performance.

oxidative.q: What is the final electron acceptor in the ETC, and what does it form?
oxidative.a: Oxygen (O₂); it accepts electrons at the end of the chain and forms water.

oxidative.q: Approximate net ATP yield from complete oxidation of one blood-glucose molecule?
oxidative.a: ~32 ATP. (30–36 if starting from muscle glycogen, depending on the NADH shuttle used.)

oxidative.q: How many ATP does one NADH vs one FADH₂ produce in the 5E accounting?
oxidative.a: NADH ≈ 2.5 ATP, FADH₂ ≈ 1.5 ATP. (Older biochemistry texts cite 3 and 2.)

oxidative.q: Calc: total the ATP for one glucose through the oxidative system using 5E values.
oxidative.a: Glycolysis 2 net (substrate-level) + 2 NADH×2.5 = 5; pyruvate ox 2 NADH×2.5 = 5; Krebs 2 GTP + 6 NADH×2.5 (=15) + 2 FADH₂×1.5 (=3). Sum = 2+5+5+2+15+3 = 32 ATP.

oxidative.q: What enzyme frees fatty acids from triglyceride stores, and where are the FFAs then oxidized?
oxidative.a: Hormone-sensitive lipase; FFAs enter the mitochondria and undergo β-oxidation to acetyl-CoA.

oxidative.q: Why does fat yield far more ATP than glucose per molecule, and what's the trade-off?
oxidative.a: Long carbon chains produce many acetyl-CoA (>100 ATP per palmitate) via β-oxidation → huge capacity, but fat oxidation requires more O₂, so it's slower per unit time.

oxidative.q: At rest, roughly what fraction of ATP comes from fat vs carbohydrate?
What happens to substrate preference as exercise intensity rises toward high-intensity aerobic work?
oxidative.a: It shifts from fat toward carbohydrate; at high-intensity aerobic work nearly 100% of energy comes from CHO if supply is adequate.

oxidative.q: During prolonged submaximal steady-state work, which way does fuel use drift and why?
oxidative.a: From CHO back toward fat, because muscle/liver glycogen depletes over time; protein contributes only a small amount.

oxidative.q: Name the rate-limiting step of the Krebs cycle and its regulation.
oxidative.a: Isocitrate → α-ketoglutarate, catalyzed by isocitrate dehydrogenase; stimulated by ADP, allosterically inhibited by ATP.

oxidative.q: Rate vs capacity: rank phosphagen, glycolysis, and fat oxidation.
oxidative.a: Phosphagen = highest rate (but lowest capacity); fat oxidation = greatest capacity (but slowest rate). An inverse relationship holds across systems.

oxidative.q: Applied: an athlete pushes their lactate threshold to the right after aerobic training — what oxidative adaptation drives this?
oxidative.a: Increased mitochondrial content → greater aerobic ATP production and more reliance on FFAs, so they hold a higher % VO₂max before blood lactate accumulates.

oxidative.q: What percentage of ATP synthesis does oxidative phosphorylation account for vs substrate-level phosphorylation?
oxidative.a: Over 90%, showing the oxidative system's dominant capacity for energy transfer.

oxidative.q: Applied: for a fat-oxidation training block, would you program low steady-state or repeated high-intensity intervals, and why?
oxidative.a: Low-intensity steady-state — it keeps you below the crossover point where fat is the dominant substrate and stimulates mitochondrial adaptations, whereas high intensity forces near-total CHO oxidation.

energy_system_interaction.q: What determines each energy system's contribution — intensity or duration?
energy_system_interaction.a: Primarily intensity, secondarily duration. No single system ever supplies 100% of the energy, at rest or in exercise.

energy_system_interaction.q: State the rate-vs-capacity trade-off and name the extremes.
energy_system_interaction.a: Inverse: higher rate → lower capacity. Phosphagen = highest rate, lowest capacity; fat oxidation = lowest rate, highest capacity.

energy_system_interaction.q: Which substrates, when depleted, actually limit performance?
energy_system_interaction.a: Phosphagens (ATP/CP) and glycogen. FFAs, lactate, and amino acids are rarely limiting.

energy_system_interaction.q: Give the PCr recovery timeline after intense exercise.
energy_system_interaction.a: ~50% resynthesized in 30s; ATP fully back in ~3-5 min; CP fully back by ~8 min — accomplished mainly aerobically.

energy_system_interaction.q: During intense exercise, why does muscle ATP stay relatively constant while CP crashes?
energy_system_interaction.a: CP donates phosphate via creatine kinase to resynthesize ATP; myokinase and oxidation of glycogen/FFAs add further ATP, defending ATP concentration.

energy_system_interaction.q: Define O₂ deficit and EPOC and state whether they are equal.
energy_system_interaction.a: O₂ deficit = anaerobic energy covering the onset lag; EPOC = post-exercise O₂ above rest restoring homeostasis. Related but NOT equal.

energy_system_interaction.q: What is the single biggest driver of EPOC magnitude?
energy_system_interaction.a: Exercise intensity. Duration and mode matter too, but intensity has the greatest effect (heavy resistance > light circuit).

energy_system_interaction.q: List four processes EPOC pays for.
energy_system_interaction.a: Reloading O₂ in blood/muscle, ATP+CP resynthesis, elevated temperature/circulation/ventilation, and increased triglyceride-FFA cycling / protein turnover.

energy_system_interaction.q: Athlete does 5s all-out sled sprints for phosphagen power. What work:rest ratio?
energy_system_interaction.a: ~1:12 to 1:20 (e.g., 5-10s work, 90-100% power), because full PCr recovery is slow and aerobic.

energy_system_interaction.q: At 100% VO₂max, muscle glycogenolysis runs ~3.4 mmol·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹. Over a 5-min effort in ~10 kg active muscle, roughly how much glycogen is used?
energy_system_interaction.a: 3.4 × 10 × 5 ≈ 170 mmol glucosyl units. Point: high intensity burns glycogen fast — order-of-magnitude estimate.

energy_system_interaction.q: Athlete cramps a 24h day between a heavy metcon and a race. What CHO refuel maximizes glycogen?
energy_system_interaction.a: 0.7-3.0 g CHO/kg every 2h post-exercise → replenishes ~5-6 mmol/g wet muscle/h over the first 4-6h; near-full by 24h.

energy_system_interaction.q: In cycle ergometry, at what effort duration does aerobic metabolism become the primary energy supplier?
energy_system_interaction.a: Beyond ~60s. Anaerobic mechanisms are the primary contributors up to ~60s (roughly a 50/50 split near 60s), after which aerobic metabolism progressively takes over.

energy_system_interaction.q: Why is long slow distance a poor primary method for a Hyrox/team-sport athlete?
energy_system_interaction.a: Their events demand repeated efforts above sustainable aerobic power. Building only aerobic efficiency while neglecting anaerobic power/capacity gives little event-specific benefit — interval training with matched work:rest is more specific.

biomech_levers.q: Define torque and give its SI unit.
biomech_levers.a: Torque (moment) = force × moment arm (the perpendicular distance from the force's line of action to the joint axis). SI unit is the newton-metre (N·m).

biomech_levers.q: In a biceps curl, what class of lever is the forearm and what is its mechanical advantage?
biomech_levers.a: Third-class lever — muscle and load on the same side of the elbow, muscle moment arm shorter than the load's. MA <1, so biceps force must exceed the resistive force.
1st: muscle and load on opposite sides of fulcrum (triceps). 2nd: same side, muscle moment arm longer, MA>1 (calf raise). 3rd: same side, muscle moment arm shorter, MA<1 (biceps curl).

biomech_levers.q: Why are internal muscle and tendon forces usually far greater than the external load lifted?
biomech_levers.a: Most joints are 3rd-class levers with MA <1: the muscle moment arm is shorter than the resistive moment arm, so the muscle must generate much more force than the load to produce equal torque.

biomech_levers.q: Fig 2.1 (elbow extension): muscle moment arm 5 cm, resistive moment arm 40 cm. Compute the mechanical advantage.
biomech_levers.a: MA = 5 ÷ 40 = 0.125 (< 1.0), a mechanical disadvantage — the triceps force must be 8× the resistive force.

biomech_levers.q: A load hangs on a 40 cm resistive moment arm; the muscle acts at a 5 cm moment arm. To hold a 100 N load, what muscle force is needed?
biomech_levers.a: Balance torques: F_m × 0.05 = 100 × 0.40 → F_m = 40/0.05 = 800 N. The muscle needs 8× the load.

biomech_levers.q: What does the patella do for the quadriceps mechanically?
biomech_levers.a: It holds the quadriceps tendon away from the knee's axis of rotation, keeping the quad moment arm long and preserving its mechanical advantage through the ROM.

biomech_levers.q: Trade-off of a tendon inserted farther from the joint centre?
biomech_levers.a: ↑Moment arm → more torque/force for a given muscle force (good for slow, heavy lifts), but less joint rotation per unit of muscle shortening → lower movement speed.

biomech_levers.q: In a biceps curl, at what forearm position is resistive torque greatest, and where is it zero?
biomech_levers.a: Greatest when the forearm is horizontal (weight's horizontal distance to elbow is maximal); zero when the weight sits directly above or below the elbow axis.

biomech_levers.q: Why is understanding mechanical advantage more useful than classifying a lever?
biomech_levers.a: The lever class depends on where you place the fulcrum, which is somewhat arbitrary in real movement; MA directly tells you the force/torque relationship regardless of the label.

biomech_levers.q: How does a low-bar vs high-bar squat change the resistive torque distribution?
biomech_levers.a: Low-bar → more forward trunk lean → weight horizontally closer to knees (↓knee torque) but farther from hips (↑hip/glute-ham torque). High-bar → the opposite: ↑knee torque, ↓hip torque.

biomech_levers.q: What is a Nautilus-style cam meant to do, and why does it often fail?
biomech_levers.a: A variable-radius cam changes the weight-stack moment arm to match the muscle's torque curve — more resistance where you're strong, less where you're weak. It requires a constant slow angular velocity to work and frequently doesn't match real human torque patterns.

biomech_levers.q: Why do longer limbs demand more muscle torque for the same external load?
biomech_levers.a: A longer limb places the load on a longer resistive moment arm, so the resistive torque (load × moment arm) is larger and the muscle must produce more torque to balance it.

force_velocity_length_tension.q: Order the three muscle actions from greatest to least force capability.
Per Hill's force–velocity relationship, where along the velocity range is the drop in concentric force steepest?
force_velocity_length_tension.a: Over the lower (slow) range of movement speeds — the curve is non-linear.

force_velocity_length_tension.q: Up to what angular velocity does eccentric torque keep increasing before it declines?
force_velocity_length_tension.a: About 90°/s (~1.57 rad/s), after which it declines gradually.

force_velocity_length_tension.q: Why does muscle force peak at resting length?
force_velocity_length_tension.a: Actin and myosin overlap gives the maximal number of potential crossbridge sites; stretching reduces aligned filaments and over-shortening causes excessive overlap, both cutting crossbridges.

force_velocity_length_tension.q: What role does titin play during eccentric actions?
force_velocity_length_tension.a: It is a mechanosensing elastic filament that tightens with rising calcium, aligning myosin to add crossbridge sites, aiding force production and signaling hypertrophy.

force_velocity_length_tension.q: Why must muscle force be considered as torque rather than raw force?
force_velocity_length_tension.a: All body movement occurs by rotation about joints, so a muscle's effect equals its force times its moment arm (torque).

force_velocity_length_tension.q: A first-class lever has a muscle moment arm of 5 cm and a resistive moment arm of 40 cm. What is the mechanical advantage, and what does it mean?
force_velocity_length_tension.a: MA = 5/40 = 0.125 (<1.0), a disadvantage: muscle force must far exceed the resistive force to balance the torque.

force_velocity_length_tension.q: If a resistive moment arm is 8× the muscle moment arm, how large must muscle force be relative to the load?
force_velocity_length_tension.a: 8× the resistive force (torque balance: FM×MM = FR×MR).

force_velocity_length_tension.q: Classify a heel raise by lever class and explain its advantage.
force_velocity_length_tension.a: Second-class lever: muscle and resistive forces on the same side of the fulcrum with the muscle moment arm longer (MM > MR), so required muscle force is less than body weight — mechanical advantage.

force_velocity_length_tension.q: Which lever class describes most limb muscles, and what is the consequence?
force_velocity_length_tension.a: Third-class (MM < MR, MA < 1.0), so internal muscle forces greatly exceed the external load they move.

force_velocity_length_tension.q: Why is the bottom of a deep squat weaker than mid-range?
force_velocity_length_tension.a: Length–tension: stretched muscle has fewer aligned crossbridge sites, and the resistive moment arm/leverage often worsens at the deepest joint angles.

force_velocity_length_tension.q: During free-weight lifting, why does the resistive torque change through the range of motion?
force_velocity_length_tension.a: The resistive moment arm equals the horizontal distance from the load to the joint, which varies as the limb rotates — creating sticking points where that distance is greatest.

force_velocity_length_tension.q: How does leaning back at a biceps-curl sticking point let a lifter keep moving the bar?
force_velocity_length_tension.a: It shifts the elbow flexors toward isometric/eccentric action, where they can exert greater force than the concentric limit, pushing past the sticking point.

anabolic_hormones.q: Name the three primary anabolic hormones and their main production sites.
anabolic_hormones.a: Testosterone (testes/Leydig + adrenal cortex), growth hormone (anterior-pituitary somatotrophs), IGF-I (liver + local muscle).

anabolic_hormones.q: Which GH isoform is exercise-sensitive, and what stimulates its release?
anabolic_hormones.a: The 22 kD (191-aa) isoform; metabolic stress — rising lactate and falling pH — is the key stimulus, so high total work + short rest drive it.

anabolic_hormones.q: Why can local muscle IGF-I matter more than circulating IGF-I?
anabolic_hormones.a: Mechanical load makes muscle fibers produce IGF-I (incl. the MGF splice variant) that acts autocrine/paracrine directly on the cell; circulating IGF-I may not consistently change with resistance exercise.

anabolic_hormones.q: List the protocol features that maximize an acute testosterone spike.
anabolic_hormones.a: Large muscle-group lifts (squat, deadlift, power clean), heavy load 85-95% 1RM, moderate-to-high volume, short rest 30-60s.

anabolic_hormones.q: Does acute or chronic resting hormone level correlate better with hypertrophy?
anabolic_hormones.a: The acute exercise-induced rise; resting concentrations change little with training and don't directly predict muscle gains.

anabolic_hormones.q: Which produces a greater acute hormonal response: 3×5 squat @90% with 3 min rest, or 4×10 squat @75% with 60s rest?
anabolic_hormones.a: The 4×10 — higher total work, large muscle mass, short rest and greater metabolic stress (lactate/pH) all favor the acute T/GH spike.

anabolic_hormones.q: Your athlete uses heavy 1-3RM triples with >3 min rest and sees little blood-hormone rise. Is the session useless for growth?
anabolic_hormones.a: No — low metabolic demand means small hormonal change, but max motor-unit recruitment + enhanced receptor binding still drive strength; slight hormonal shifts plus autocrine/paracrine signaling suffice.

anabolic_hormones.q: Why does pre/post carb+protein feeding lower measured blood testosterone yet still help?
anabolic_hormones.a: Greater muscle uptake reduces circulating T, but the feeding upregulates skeletal-muscle androgen-receptor content — improving how the available T is used.

anabolic_hormones.q: What is receptor downregulation, and give a classic example.
anabolic_hormones.a: Chronically high hormone desensitizes or reduces receptor number, cutting response. Classic example: insulin resistance from sustained high insulin.

anabolic_hormones.q: Roughly what fraction of total testosterone is free, and why does it matter?
anabolic_hormones.a: About 0.5-2% is free (unbound); per the free-hormone hypothesis only the free fraction directly interacts with muscle tissue.

anabolic_hormones.q: How does GH indirectly and directly support training adaptation?
anabolic_hormones.a: Indirectly: stimulates liver IGF-I → protein synthesis. Directly: promotes lipolysis, sparing muscle glycogen, plus collagen synthesis and immune-cell support.

anabolic_hormones.q: Circulating testosterone in women is roughly what fraction of men's, and does exercise raise it much?
anabolic_hormones.a: About 5-10% of male levels; acute total-T rises are usually small or absent, though free-T and androgen-receptor responses can still occur.

catabolic_hormones.q: Name the primary glucocorticoid and its gland of origin.
Which adrenal region secretes catecholamines, and by what stimulus?
catabolic_hormones.a: The adrenal medulla, via direct sympathetic (autonomic) neural stimulation.

catabolic_hormones.q: What hormone signals the adrenal cortex to release cortisol, and from where?
catabolic_hormones.a: ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone) from the anterior pituitary, part of the HPA axis.
No — acute rises are adaptive (glucose sparing, gluconeogenesis, fat oxidation). The problem is chronic elevation that fails to return to baseline within ~24 h.

catabolic_hormones.q: List three catabolic/anti-anabolic effects of chronically elevated cortisol.
catabolic_hormones.a: ↓protein synthesis, ↑protein degradation, immune suppression (also blunts anabolic signaling).

catabolic_hormones.q: Name three physiological effects of catecholamines during resistance exercise.
catabolic_hormones.a: ↑force production, ↑contraction rate, ↑blood pressure/blood flow via vasodilation; they also augment testosterone secretion.

catabolic_hormones.q: Which resistance protocol variables maximize the serum cortisol response?
catabolic_hormones.a: High volume, large muscle groups, and short rest periods.

catabolic_hormones.q: Why does epinephrine correlate with blood lactate?
catabolic_hormones.a: Epinephrine drives glycogenolysis and metabolic stress; lactate rises with the same high-intensity work, so it tracks catecholamine involvement in the metabolic response.

catabolic_hormones.q: What adaptation lets trained athletes limit cortisol's catabolic exposure?
catabolic_hormones.a: Enhanced conversion of cortisol to cortisone, reducing active glucocorticoid exposure after exercise.

catabolic_hormones.q: How does heavy resistance overtraining affect catecholamine signaling?
catabolic_hormones.a: It downregulates β2-receptors, reducing epinephrine's effect and lowering muscle contractile force; medullary output can also decline (adrenal exhaustion).

catabolic_hormones.q: A masters athlete runs dense high-volume blocks with poor sleep and no rest days. What endocrine risk are you managing?
catabolic_hormones.a: Chronically elevated cortisol staying above baseline past 24 h → catabolic state (protein loss, immune suppression) plus adrenal exhaustion; prescribe rest days and lower-volume weeks.

catabolic_hormones.q: How should programming be structured to protect adrenal recovery?
catabolic_hormones.a: Vary intensity/volume and rest-period length over time, include complete rest days and lower-volume sessions to let the adrenal glands recover and avoid nonfunctional overreaching/overtraining.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: In an untrained lifter's first ~4 weeks, what adaptation type dominates strength gains?
anaerobic_adaptations.a: Neural — ↑recruitment, ↑firing rate/rate coding, ↑synchronization, ↓GTO inhibition and antagonist coactivation, before measurable hypertrophy.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: Per Sale, when does hypertrophy overtake neural factors as the main driver of strength?
anaerobic_adaptations.a: After roughly 10 weeks; neural gains dominate ~6-10 wks, then structural changes contribute more.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: State Henneman's size principle and its Type I/II order.
anaerobic_adaptations.a: Motor units recruit in ascending soma size — low-threshold (Type I) first, high-threshold (Type II, more force but fatigable) last as force demand climbs.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: Which fiber type hypertrophies more with heavy resistance training, and roughly when is CSA change detectable?
anaerobic_adaptations.a: Type II grows more than Type I; CSA change becomes measurable at ~3-4 weeks (9-12 workouts).

anaerobic_adaptations.q: Describe the early fiber-type transition with training and what detraining does to it.
anaerobic_adaptations.a: IIx shifts toward IIa (more oxidative MHC) early — e.g. IIx ~18%→7% over 8 wks. Detraining reverses it, sometimes overshooting IIx above pretraining.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: What is the minimal essential strain (MES) as a fraction of fracture force?
anaerobic_adaptations.a: About 1/10 of the force required to fracture the bone.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: Why is tendon injury risk elevated when load is ramped too fast?
anaerobic_adaptations.a: Connective tissue (tendon, ligament, bone) adapts far slower than muscle — muscle strength can outpace tendon capacity, and stiffness only rises with heavy loads.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: Do resting hormone concentrations change chronically with resistance training? What adapts instead?
anaerobic_adaptations.a: Resting testosterone/GH/IGF-I/cortisol are largely unchanged; the adaptation is an altered ACUTE hormonal response plus androgen-receptor content changes.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: How does the cardiac adaptation to resistance training differ from aerobic training?
anaerobic_adaptations.a: RT causes concentric LV wall thickening/↑mass with little chamber-volume change; aerobic training enlarges chamber volume (eccentric). RT effect shrinks when normalized to lean mass/BSA.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: In detraining, which is lost first — neural drive or muscle CSA — and how long is strength held?
anaerobic_adaptations.a: Neural losses come first, atrophy later; strength is generally maintained ~4 weeks, and muscle memory speeds re-gain.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: A coach designs a program to raise hip/spine BMD. Give the key loading rules.
anaerobic_adaptations.a: Multijoint structural lifts directing force axially through spine/hip (squat, clean, deadlift), heavy + ballistic/high-impact loading, multidirectional, progressive overload — ~30-35 reps suffices per session.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: By ~5 months of resistance training, roughly how much do CP, ATP, and glycogen stores rise (MacDougall)?
anaerobic_adaptations.a: CP +28%, ATP +18%, glycogen up to +112% via supercompensation.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: What is cross-education, and roughly how much untrained-limb strength gain does it yield?
anaerobic_adaptations.a: Training one limb raises strength/EMG in the untrained contralateral limb (central neural). Average ~8% gain (up to 22%), load-dependent, with no CSA change in the untrained limb.

anaerobic_adaptations.q: How does concurrent high-intensity aerobic training interfere with anaerobic gains?
anaerobic_adaptations.a: It blunts power and rate of force development more than slow-velocity strength (interference effect), worsened by high aerobic intensity/volume/frequency and short recovery; aerobic power is largely unharmed by RT.

aerobic_adaptations.q: Decompose VO₂max into its central and peripheral determinants.
aerobic_adaptations.a: VO₂max = max cardiac output × max a-vO₂ difference. Q (central) = HR × SV. The a-vO₂ difference (peripheral) reflects O₂ extraction by capillaries and mitochondria.

aerobic_adaptations.q: Which single variable most increases max cardiac output with training, and what happens to max HR?
aerobic_adaptations.a: Stroke volume — via larger LV chamber/wall and greater contractility. Max HR stays unchanged or decreases slightly.

aerobic_adaptations.q: After ~6-12 months, which keeps improving: VO₂max or lactate threshold?
aerobic_adaptations.a: Lactate threshold (and economy). VO₂max plateaus; LT continues to rise over years, letting you hold a higher %VO₂max.

aerobic_adaptations.q: Why does resting HR drop to 40-60 in trained endurance athletes?
aerobic_adaptations.a: Increased parasympathetic (vagal) tone slows SA-node discharge, plus higher SV means fewer beats needed for the same resting cardiac output.

aerobic_adaptations.q: What is the typical range of VO₂max improvement, and what determines where an athlete lands in it?
aerobic_adaptations.a: ~5-30% overall, set by starting fitness and genetic ceiling — untrained gain most (the 5E cites up to ~20% in untrained individuals), while elite athletes show minimal change.

aerobic_adaptations.q: Two runners both have VO₂max 50 mL/kg/min. One's LT is at 80%, the other's at 70%. What's the sustainable-intensity gap?
aerobic_adaptations.a: 80% = 40 vs 70% = 35 mL/kg/min → the first can sustain ~5 mL/kg/min higher, meaning a faster raceable pace at equal economy.

aerobic_adaptations.q: What happens to hemoglobin mass vs hematocrit with aerobic training?
aerobic_adaptations.a: Hemoglobin mass increases, but hematocrit (%) may dip slightly because plasma volume rises even more, diluting red-cell concentration.

aerobic_adaptations.q: By how much does VO₂max fall with short-term vs long-term detraining, and why?
aerobic_adaptations.a: ~4-14% short-term (<4 wk), ~6-20% long-term (>4 wk). Driven by ↓blood volume, ↓SV, ↓max Q, and ↑submaximal HR.

aerobic_adaptations.q: How does the 5E describe muscle fiber SIZE changes under high aerobic volume?
aerobic_adaptations.a: Fiber size can decrease (~20% after marathon training in both fiber types) while functional strength is maintained or improves — a more oxidative phenotype.
Detraining = unplanned loss of adaptations from insufficient stimulus (reversibility). Tapering = planned reduction in volume (not intensity) before competition, designed to enhance performance.

aerobic_adaptations.q: Why does running build bone but cycling/swimming largely don't?
aerobic_adaptations.a: Running is high-impact weight-bearing (forces several × bodyweight) exceeding the bone-remodeling threshold; cycling/swimming are weight-supported and can even reduce non-locomotor bone density.

aerobic_adaptations.q: How does aerobic training shift substrate use, and why does that aid endurance?
aerobic_adaptations.a: ↑fat oxidation (more fat-metabolizing enzymes + mitochondrial capacity) spares muscle glycogen, letting you hold a given intensity longer before depletion.

aerobic_adaptations.q: The 5E notes a downside of multisport training for neuromuscular adaptation — what is it?
aerobic_adaptations.a: Triathletes show less refined, more variable muscle coactivation than single-sport athletes, reflecting interference in neuromuscular adaptation.

age_sex.q: In preadolescents, are training-induced strength gains primarily neural or hypertrophic? Why?
age_sex.a: Primarily NEURAL (↑motor-unit activation, recruitment, firing frequency, coordination). Low circulating androgens (testosterone ~20-60 ng/100mL) limit hypertrophy before puberty.

age_sex.q: True or false: Resistance training stunts growth in children.
age_sex.a: False. With supervision and proper technique it is safe and actually stimulates bone remodeling; no growth-plate damage in guideline-adhering studies.

age_sex.q: Approximate PHV ages for girls vs boys, and why does PHV matter for programming?
age_sex.a: ~11.9 yr girls, ~13.8 yr boys. Injury risk rises around PHV (altered center of mass, muscle imbalances, tight muscle-tendon units); may need to reduce volume/intensity and reinforce technique.
Sarcopenia = age-related loss of muscle MASS/function; dynapenia = age-related loss of STRENGTH. Related but not identical.

age_sex.q: Which declines faster with age — muscle strength or muscle power? Coaching implication?
age_sex.a: POWER declines faster. Program explicit power/high-velocity work for older adults since ADLs (stair climbing, rising from chair) need rapid force.

age_sex.q: Define the BMD thresholds for osteopenia and osteoporosis.
age_sex.a: Osteopenia = BMD −1 to −2.5 SD below young-adult mean; osteoporosis = below −2.5 SD.

age_sex.q: How does the female-to-male strength gap change when normalized to fat-free mass?
age_sex.a: It largely DISAPPEARS. Absolute gaps trace to body composition and lean-mass distribution; lower-body relative strength is close to male values, upper body still somewhat less.

age_sex.q: Does the 5E support tailoring resistance-training prescription to menstrual-cycle phase?
age_sex.a: No. Current meta-analyses find no systematic phase-performance relationship; the 5E does not support modifying RT prescription by cycle phase.

age_sex.q: Why can two 14-year-olds of the same chronological age need very different programs?
age_sex.a: Biological maturation can differ by up to ~5 years. Early vs late maturers differ in size, strength, and injury risk, so program by maturity/training age/technical competency, not birthdate.

age_sex.q: A previously sedentary 80-year-old wants to start lifting. What early-phase priorities and safety points apply?
age_sex.a: Start low intensity/volume, machines early for balance, emphasize technique/confidence/education, AVOID Valsalva, 48-72h recovery, 2-3x/wk; progress toward 2-3 sets of 6-12 reps at 70-85% 1RM plus power work.

age_sex.q: Why is upper-body absolute strength further from male values than lower-body in females?
age_sex.a: Lean-mass distribution: males carry proportionally more upper-body muscle (broader shoulders). Lower body is closer; relative to fat-free mass, both gaps shrink.

age_sex.q: What accounts for females' greater fatigue resistance in prolonged endurance events despite lower VO2max/kg?
age_sex.a: Greater proportion of type I oxidative fibers, higher fat oxidation (sparing carbohydrate), and ~33% greater mitochondrial respiration.

age_sex.q: Roughly what strength gain do untrained preadolescents show over an 8-20 week program, and how does it compare to adolescents?
age_sex.a: ~30-40% typical (range ~10-90% reported). Adolescents attain ~50% larger ABSOLUTE strength gains than children due to hormonal maturation.

age_sex.q: Why is RT considered a lifelong commitment in both youth and seniors?
age_sex.a: Training-induced strength/BMD gains are impermanent — they detrain toward baseline in youth and revert in seniors (BMD can be lost after >6 months off), so a maintenance stimulus must continue.

sport_psych.q: Define arousal and name three physiological ways it is indexed.
sport_psych.a: Arousal = blended physiological + psychological activation on a continuum (deep sleep → intense excitement); reflects intensity of motivation, not valence. Indexed via HR, BP, EEG, EMG, or catecholamine levels.

sport_psych.q: Distinguish state anxiety from trait anxiety and give the causal link.
sport_psych.a: State anxiety = transient, situational apprehension with elevated autonomic activity. Trait anxiety = a stable disposition to perceive situations as threatening. High trait anxiety primes/increases the state anxiety an athlete experiences.

sport_psych.q: Break anxiety into its two components and give an example of each.
sport_psych.a: Cognitive anxiety = worry/negative thoughts (e.g., 'I'll fail'). Somatic anxiety = physical symptoms (tense muscles, racing heart, upset stomach).

sport_psych.q: How does drive theory differ from the inverted-U theory?
sport_psych.a: Drive theory (Hull): arousal and performance rise linearly — more arousal is always better. Inverted-U (Yerkes-Dodson): performance peaks at moderate arousal, then declines with further increases.

sport_psych.q: How do skill level and task complexity shift the optimal arousal point?
sport_psych.a: More skill widens the tolerable arousal range and raises the optimal point; novices and complex decision-making tasks need LOWER arousal to avoid attentional overload. Simple/well-learned skills tolerate (and can benefit from) high arousal.

sport_psych.q: In what two ways does IZOF refine the inverted-U?
sport_psych.a: (1) Peak performance need not sit at the arousal midpoint; (2) optimal is a personal bandwidth/range, not a single point — and positive or negative emotions can help or hurt depending on the individual.

sport_psych.q: According to catastrophe theory, what causes a sudden performance crash rather than a gradual decline?
sport_psych.a: Rising somatic (physiological) arousal in the presence of HIGH cognitive anxiety triggers an abrupt collapse; simply calming down afterward does not fully restore the prior performance level.

sport_psych.q: What is reversal theory's key claim about arousal?
sport_psych.a: Kerr: the EFFECT of arousal depends on how the athlete interprets it — as excitement/readiness vs dread/threat. Athletes can consciously reverse the interpretation, so arousal's impact is within their control.

sport_psych.q: Contrast MAS- and MAF-dominated athletes in the tasks they prefer.
sport_psych.a: MAS (motive to achieve success): drawn to uncertain/challenging tasks near ~50% success odds to test themselves. MAF (motive to avoid failure): pick very easy or very hard tasks to dodge the shame of failing.
Performance accomplishments (past success — strongest), vicarious experience (modeling), verbal persuasion, imaginal experience (imagery), physiological states, and emotional states.

sport_psych.q: Why favor process goals over outcome goals for skill acquisition?
sport_psych.a: Process goals target controllable execution, so success tracks effort, builds confidence, and directs attention to task-relevant cues. Outcome goals depend on others and, when over-emphasized, narrow attention and slow reaction — hurting the very result sought.

sport_psych.q: Name Fitts & Posner's three stages of motor learning.
sport_psych.a: Cognitive (effortful, conscious thought) → associative (focused but less detail-bound) → autonomous/automaticity (relaxed, automatic execution with irrelevant cues filtered out).

sport_psych.q: Why can instructional self-talk hurt an expert performer?
sport_psych.a: Feeding explicit performance cues to an expert can interfere with the automaticity of a well-learned movement, disrupting fluid execution.

sport_psych.q: How does diaphragmatic breathing lower arousal physiologically?
sport_psych.a: Deep, rhythmic belly breathing shifts autonomic balance toward parasympathetic dominance and vagal tone (via brainstem respiratory-cardiac feedback), lowering heart rate and muscle tension — countering the sympathetic fight-or-flight response.

macros.q: kcal per gram: carbohydrate, protein, fat, alcohol.
How many amino acids are essential, nonessential, and conditionally essential?

macros.a: RDA 0.8 g/kg/day. Endurance athletes 1.2-2.0 g/kg; strength/muscle-building 1.4-2.0 g/kg. Reduced-calorie phases may need the high end to spare muscle.

macros.q: Per-feeding protein dose and frequency for maximal MPS.
macros.a: 20-40 g high-quality protein (~0.25-0.40 g/kg) every 3-4 hr. Total daily protein matters more than exact timing.

macros.q: Where is glycogen stored and roughly how much?
macros.a: ~15 g/kg body weight; three-quarters in skeletal muscle, one-quarter in liver.

macros.q: Why is carbohydrate not technically an essential nutrient?
macros.a: The body can make glucose from amino-acid carbon skeletons via gluconeogenesis, so dietary CHO isn't strictly required (though it's the main high-intensity fuel).

macros.q: A food has GI 70 and 30 g carbohydrate per serving. Glycemic load?
Adequate Intake for water, men vs women.
macros.a: Men 3.7 L/day, women 2.7 L/day (from all fluids and foods).

macros.q: A 75 kg athlete trains 12 hr/wk. Carbohydrate target?
macros.a: High-volume endurance 6-10 g/kg/day → ~450-750 g CHO; push toward the top on heavy/double days.

macros.q: An athlete loses 1.5 kg of body weight over a session. How much fluid to fully rehydrate?
macros.a: ~1.5 L per kg lost → ~2.25 L of fluid with electrolytes (accounts for extra urine output).

macros.q: What sodium and carbohydrate concentration does the IOM recommend for a sport drink in prolonged hot-weather exercise?
macros.a: 20-30 mEq sodium/L (460-690 mg), 2-5 mEq potassium/L, and 5-10% carbohydrate.

macros.q: Why can heavy sweaters drinking only water develop hyponatremia?
macros.a: Replacing large sweat (and sodium) losses with plain water dilutes blood sodium <130 mmol/L → cell swelling, cramps, and at <120 mmol/L seizures/coma.

macros.q: Compare heme and nonheme iron absorption.
macros.a: Heme (animal) ~15-35% absorbed, unaffected by other foods; nonheme (plant) only 2-20%, lowered by phytates/tannins/calcium and raised by vitamin C.

macros.q: How much body-weight loss from dehydration measurably impairs performance?
macros.a: As little as 2-3% BW loss raises core temp and cuts power, strength, endurance, and focus; keep losses under 2%.

nutrient_timing.q: Pre-competition CHO and protein for a meal eaten ≥4 h out?
nutrient_timing.a: 1-4 g/kg CHO + 0.15-0.25 g/kg protein; low fat/fiber, familiar foods. At 2 h out, drop to ~1 g/kg CHO.

nutrient_timing.q: Why combine glucose and fructose during long events?
nutrient_timing.a: Each sugar has its own intestinal transporter, so mixing types (e.g., glucose + fructose or maltodextrin) pushes absorption/oxidation past the ~1 g/min single-sugar cap without saturating one transporter — the way to reach the top of the 28-144 g/hr band.

nutrient_timing.q: Ideal sport-drink composition for prolonged hot-weather exercise?
nutrient_timing.a: CHO 5-10% (6-8% ideal), sodium 20-30 mEq/L, potassium 2-5 mEq/L. Above 8% CHO delays gastric emptying.

nutrient_timing.q: Post-exercise CHO rate to maximize glycogen resynthesis, and the two-phase timeline?
nutrient_timing.a: 1.0-1.85 g/kg/hr. Fast, insulin-independent phase lasts ~30-60 min; a slower insulin-dependent phase follows for several hours.

nutrient_timing.q: When does adding protein speed glycogen replenishment?
nutrient_timing.a: When CHO intake is below ~1.2 g/kg/hr — co-ingested protein raises the glycogen storage rate. Above that CHO rate it adds little to glycogen.

nutrient_timing.q: Post-resistance protein dose and the key driver of the MPS response?
nutrient_timing.a: 20-50 g high-quality protein (older adults ≥40 g). Leucine content is the driver — ~2-3 g leucine (or 0.05 g/kg) per serving maximally stimulates MPS.

nutrient_timing.q: CHO-loading protocol and its glycogen effect?
nutrient_timing.a: ~8-10 g/kg/day (up to 10-12 pre-marathon) with taper for ~3 days → muscle glycogen ↑ 20-40% above normal. Benefits continuous efforts >90 min.

nutrient_timing.q: How wide is the 'anabolic window,' and when does post-exercise timing actually matter most?
nutrient_timing.a: Wider than once thought (a 'garage door'). Timing matters most when the athlete trains again in <24 h; with >24 h recovery, total daily intake over 24 h suffices.

nutrient_timing.q: Daily protein targets: endurance vs strength/power vs hypertrophy/weight gain?
nutrient_timing.a: Endurance 1.4-2.0, strength/power 1.4-2.0, hypertrophy/weight gain 1.6-2.2 g/kg/day. Spread as 20-40 g per meal every 3-4 h.

nutrient_timing.q: Calc: energy availability for a 60 kg athlete (18% BF) eating 2,400 kcal who burns 700 kcal in exercise — flag it.
nutrient_timing.a: FFM = 60 × 0.82 = 49.2 kg. EA = (2400 − 700)/49.2 ≈ 34.6 kcal/kg FFM/day. Below the ~45 optimal and near the <30 danger zone — low EA / RED-S risk; under-fueled.

nutrient_timing.q: Calc: CHO for a 70 kg cyclist over a 3 h ride at 80 g/hr — total, and is it in range?
nutrient_timing.a: 3 × 80 = 240 g total. 80 g/hr sits inside the 5E's 28-144 g/hr during-event band, but requires mixed sugars (glucose+fructose/maltodextrin) to exceed the ~1 g/min single-sugar oxidation ceiling.

nutrient_timing.q: Define energy availability and its optimal vs low-EA thresholds.
nutrient_timing.a: EA = (energy intake − exercise energy expenditure) / kg FFM. ~45 kcal/kg FFM/day is optimal; <30 marks low EA and RED-S risk.
Rinsing CHO without swallowing improves ~1 h performance by 2-3%, presumably via CNS/oral receptors — no gut absorption needed.

nutrient_timing.q: Below what body-weight fluid loss should athletes stay during exercise?
nutrient_timing.a: Keep sweat-driven water loss under 2% of body weight to protect performance and prevent heat illness.

peds.q: What agency sets the international annual prohibited-substances list, and who enforces it nationally?
peds.a: WADA (World Anti-Doping Agency); national bodies like USADA, UKAD, and Sport Integrity Australia enforce it. NCAA maintains its own separate list.

peds.q: By what mechanism do anabolic-androgenic steroids increase muscle mass?
peds.a: They bind androgen receptors → ↑ muscle protein synthesis → ↑ lean body mass and strength; androgenic (masculinizing) effects are inseparable.

peds.q: Why are oral 17α-alkylated steroids particularly hepatotoxic?
peds.a: The 17α-alkyl group resists first-pass hepatic breakdown (enabling oral bioavailability) but stresses the liver — linked to cholestasis, peliosis hepatis, and hepatic tumors.
Stacking = using several agents simultaneously for additive potency; cycling = on/off blocks of several weeks; pyramiding = ramping dose up then tapering down to reduce rebound side effects.

peds.q: What did Forbes's classic dose-response study show about AAS and lean body mass?
peds.a: Total AAS dose has a logarithmic relationship to ↑ lean body mass — low doses give small effects, but LBM keeps climbing with progressively larger doses.

peds.q: How does EPO improve endurance performance, and what is the main health risk?
peds.a: ↑ erythropoiesis → ↑ RBC mass (Hb up ~5-17%, hct up ~8-19%) → ↑ O₂-carrying capacity → ↑ aerobic power/max power output ~7-10%. Risk = ↑ blood viscosity → clotting, raised systolic BP, stroke, and cerebral/pulmonary embolism (linked to cyclist deaths).

peds.q: What is the intended advantage of SARMs over traditional anabolic steroids, and is that fully realized?
peds.a: Tissue-selective AR agonism (muscle/bone) with fewer androgenic effects on prostate/liver. Not fully realized — trials still show ↓ HDL, ↓ testosterone, and ↑ ALT/hepatotoxicity signals; all remain investigational and WADA-banned.

peds.q: What condition results from GH excess after puberty, and what are the abuse-related risks?
peds.a: Acromegaly (bone widening, organ enlargement, arthritis, metabolic abnormalities). Abuse risks add insulin resistance/diabetes, cardiovascular dysfunction, hypertension, and accelerated osteoarthritis.

peds.q: An athlete finishes an AAS cycle and injects HCG. Why?
peds.a: HCG mimics luteinizing hormone → stimulates Leydig cells → restarts suppressed endogenous testosterone production after exogenous steroids are stopped.

peds.q: A marksman/archer wants steadier aim — which banned class is relevant, and why is it useless for a runner?
peds.a: β-blockers (reduce anxiety/tremor, steady fine motor control). They're ergolytic for endurance — they lower max HR, VO₂, and worsen race times.

peds.q: Why are prohormones (androstenedione, DHEA) poor ergogenic aids despite the marketing?
peds.a: They have only ~1/5-1/10 the biological activity of testosterone; studies show no reliable strength/mass gains but do raise estradiol and lower HDL — plus they're controlled substances (2004 Act, DASCA 2014).

peds.q: A bodybuilder self-injects insulin between meals. What is the acute danger?
peds.a: Severe hypoglycemia in a previously healthy athlete can cause coma or death; insulin is a potent anabolic hormone but injecting it is high-risk.

peds.q: Your Hyrox athlete wants a legal edge and worries about failing a test. What do you advise on supplement choice?
peds.a: Stick to evidence-based legal ergogenics (caffeine, creatine, β-alanine, bicarb, HMB) and use third-party batch-tested products (e.g., Informed-Sport) — contaminated supplements are a real source of inadvertent WADA positives.

peds.q: An athlete claims their AAS gains will be gone by test day if they stop early. What does the evidence say?
peds.a: No — fat-free mass gains persist for weeks to months after cessation (e.g., still elevated 6 wk post-cycle), which is exactly why unannounced year-round testing exists.

test_principles.q: Define validity and reliability, and state the relationship between them.
test_principles.a: Validity = the test measures what it claims to measure. Reliability = it yields consistent results on repeat under the same conditions. A test must be reliable to be valid, but a reliable test can still be invalid.

test_principles.q: Name the four types of validity and identify which is the overarching one.
test_principles.a: Construct (overarching), face, content, and criterion validity. Face, content, and criterion provide evidence supporting construct validity.

Convergent = high positive correlation between the test and the gold-standard measure of the SAME construct. Discriminant = low correlation between the test and a measure of a DIFFERENT construct (e.g., 1RM squat should NOT correlate with VO2max).

test_principles.q: What term is synonymous with interrater reliability?
test_principles.a: Objectivity (interrater agreement) — the degree to which different scorers agree on the same performance.

test_principles.q: List the four sources of variability that reduce test reliability.
test_principles.a: Intrasubject variability, interrater (lack of agreement between raters), intrarater (within-rater inconsistency), and failure of the test itself to give consistent results.

test_principles.q: Give the standard CSCS same-day testing order.
test_principles.a: 1) Non-fatiguing (height/weight/flexibility/skinfolds/vertical jump), 2) agility, 3) max power & strength (1RM), 4) sprints, 5) local muscular endurance, 6) anaerobic capacity, 7) aerobic capacity. Fatiguing anaerobic/aerobic ideally on a separate day.

test_principles.q: A bench-press 1RM test gives wildly different values across days. Which property is failing, and name two likely causes.
test_principles.a: Reliability. Likely causes: inconsistent warm-up/familiarization, technique drift, different scorers or scoring leniency, or unstandardized conditions. The test may still be valid (it measures strength).

test_principles.q: Why must aerobic/anaerobic capacity tests come last (or on a separate day)?
test_principles.a: They produce heavy fatigue that would confound subsequent tests. Glycolytic tests need ≥1 hr to recover; running the aerobic test earlier would invalidate every test after it.

test_principles.q: A coach uses the vertical jump to assess a hockey player's key demands. What validity problem does this raise?
test_principles.a: Poor content/movement specificity — hockey involves little vertical jumping, so the test doesn't emulate the sport's important movements. Choose a test matching the sport's biomechanics and energy system.

test_principles.q: Two scorers time a 40-yd sprint; one uses a handheld stopwatch. Why might their times systematically differ?
test_principles.a: Interrater error: handheld times run shorter because the rater has reaction-time delay pressing start (to the gun) but not stop (they see the athlete arrive). Use the same trained scorer or automatic timing for valid comparisons.

test_principles.q: How much rest is prescribed between trials near an athlete's max versus sub-max, and between different tests in a battery?
test_principles.a: 3 minutes between near-max attempts, 2 minutes between sub-max attempts, and at least 5 minutes between different tests in a battery.

test_principles.q: Testing at 7,900 ft after living at sea level: what two adjustments does the chapter require before an aerobic test?
test_principles.a: Adjust norms (VO2max ↓ ~5% per 3,000 ft above ~1,900 ft) and allow at least 10 days to acclimate before aerobic endurance testing.

test_principles.q: An athlete's VO2max is estimated from a 1.5-mile run performed at 82°F and 60% humidity. Why is the result suspect?
test_principles.a: Heat + humidity above ~80°F/50% impair endurance performance, so the run underestimates true VO2max. Standardize conditions or test indoors/treadmill.

test_principles.q: Why is a technique-intensive test valid for an experienced athlete but not a novice?
test_principles.a: For the trained athlete, poor technique won't limit the score, so it measures the target ability. A novice's undeveloped technique adds variability and confounds the result, lowering validity and reliability.

test_admin_strength.q: How many attempts should it take to reach a true 1RM after warm-up, and why the cap?
test_admin_strength.a: 3-5 attempts; beyond that, accumulated fatigue lowers the score.

test_admin_strength.q: Spotter setup: bench press vs back squat 1RM.
test_admin_strength.a: Bench = one spotter at the head end. Back squat = two spotters (one at each end of the bar) plus adjustable rack safety bars.

test_admin_strength.q: What position does the IMTP mimic, and what two primary metrics come from it?
test_admin_strength.a: The weightlifting second-pull (clean) position at mid-thigh; peak force and rate of force development (RFD).

test_admin_strength.q: IMTP trial durations for peak force vs RFD, and the retest rule.
test_admin_strength.a: 3-5 s for peak force, ~1 s for RFD; repeat trials if peak force varies >15% between attempts.

test_admin_strength.q: Difference between a countermovement jump and a squat (static) jump.
test_admin_strength.a: CMJ uses a rapid dip (dip then jump); the squat jump removes the countermovement — descend to ~110° knee, hold 2-3 s, then jump with no dip.

test_admin_strength.q: Calculate RSI: drop jump gives 0.36 m jump height and 0.18 s ground contact time.
test_admin_strength.a: RSI = 0.36 ÷ 0.18 = 2.0 (m/s). Higher = better fast SSC / leg stiffness.

test_admin_strength.q: An 80 kg athlete covers the Margaria-Kalamen 1.05 m vertical rise in 0.50 s. Power output?
Why is a jump-and-reach test result NOT interchangeable with a force-plate jump height?
test_admin_strength.a: Force plates/mats measure center-of-mass rise relative to takeoff, while jump-and-reach captures hand reach with timing/mobility error; the reference points differ, so values aren't comparable.

test_admin_strength.q: Why express 1RM relative to body mass, and name the two scaling approaches.
test_admin_strength.a: Strength depends heavily on body mass, so relative strength allows fair cross-athlete comparison. Ratio scaling (load ÷ BM) assumes proportionality; allometric scaling (BM raised to an exponent) better removes body-size bias.

test_admin_strength.q: Layout and scoring of the 300-yard shuttle.
test_admin_strength.a: Two lines 25 yd apart; 6 round trips (300 yd) as fast as possible, two trials separated by 5 min rest, times averaged to nearest 0.1 s. Tests anaerobic capacity.

test_admin_strength.q: Wingate test: duration, resistance basis, and key metrics.
test_admin_strength.a: 30-s max cycle sprint; resistance set proportional to body mass (~7.5%). Metrics: peak power (highest 5-s), mean/average power (anaerobic capacity), fatigue index.

test_admin_strength.q: Why shouldn't a load-velocity profile be used to set today's training load or estimate 1RM?
test_admin_strength.a: L-V prediction of 1RM is highly variable and overestimates it, risking loads above the athlete's capacity; test the 1RM directly instead.

test_admin_strength.q: What does a low eccentric utilization ratio suggest about an athlete?
test_admin_strength.a: CMJ height only slightly exceeds squat-jump height, so the athlete gains little from the countermovement — a target for stretch-shortening-cycle / plyometric work.

test_admin_strength.q: Why can gaining body weight hide a real power gain in a vertical jump?
test_admin_strength.a: Same jump height at a heavier body mass means the same takeoff velocity was produced by a heavier body — moving more mass to the same height requires higher power output, so power rose even though height looks unchanged.

test_admin_endurance.q: How is VO₂max estimated from a MAS test?
test_admin_endurance.a: VO₂max ≈ 3.5 × MAS, where MAS is the last speed (km/h) held for at least 2 min.

test_admin_endurance.q: Describe the 300-yd shuttle protocol and its scoring.
test_admin_endurance.a: Six round trips (50 yd each) between two lines 25 yd apart, sprinted max effort; two trials separated by 5-min rest, scored as the average of the two times to 0.1 s.

test_admin_endurance.q: Starting speeds and recovery for the Yo-Yo IR1 vs IR2.
test_admin_endurance.a: Both use 2×20 m shuttles with 10 s recovery; IR1 starts at 10 km/h, IR2 at 13 km/h. IR1 is recommended for general S&C use.

test_admin_endurance.q: Why prefer electronic timing gates over a hand stopwatch for speed/agility tests?
test_admin_endurance.a: Hand-timing reads ~0.24 s faster than electronic timing due to tester reaction delay and anticipation, adding error that can mask real change; gates also give split times.

test_admin_endurance.q: Which field method is the most valid/reliable for body fat, and what is the reference gold standard?
test_admin_endurance.a: Skinfolds (r≈0.99) are the best generally available field method; DXA is the labeled gold standard.

test_admin_endurance.q: A 25-yr-old male measures 24% BF by skinfolds. How should you report it and why?
test_admin_endurance.a: As a range, not a single number — the 5E gives a minimum span of about 6% for this case (there are no universally accepted body-comp norms and the minimum SEE for population-specific skinfold equations is at least ±5%), so a point value overstates precision.
T-test, 5-10-5 (pro agility), 5-0-5, Illinois test (also hexagon and the L-drill/3-cone).

test_admin_endurance.q: State three standardization rules for a valid skinfold measurement.
test_admin_endurance.a: Measure on dry skin before exercise; place prongs 1-2 cm from the pinch and read to 0.5 mm 1-2 s after release; take two trials and average if within 10% (using the correct population-specific site/equation).

test_admin_endurance.q: Why must BIA be done first thing in the morning, fasted, and post-void?
test_admin_endurance.a: BIA is highly sensitive to hydration status; standardizing conditions (AM, fasted, voided, pre-exercise) minimizes fluid-shift error so repeat tests are comparable.

test_admin_endurance.q: The hexagon test consists of how many total jumps, and how is it scored?
test_admin_endurance.a: 18 double-leg hops (3 revolutions × 6 sides) back to center; best of three trials recorded to 0.1 s.

test_admin_endurance.q: Why is the 12-min Cooper run a weaker monitoring tool than MAS or Yo-Yo IR for a trained hybrid athlete?
test_admin_endurance.a: It estimates a single VO₂max value with a pacing/ceiling that changes little in trained athletes; MAS gives a directly usable speed anchor and Yo-Yo IR captures intermittent capacity more specifically.

test_admin_endurance.q: For a straight-line sprint, what does a 10-yd split reflect versus a 40-yd time?
test_admin_endurance.a: The 10-yd split reflects acceleration; the 40-yd time reflects maximal speed. Multiple gates yield splits for both.

warmup.q: Name the three phases of the RAMP protocol in order.
What two categories of physiological effect does a warm-up produce?
warmup.a: Temperature-related (↑ muscle/core temp, ↓ viscosity, Bohr-effect O2 release, disrupted connective bonds) and non-temperature-related (↑ blood flow, ↑ baseline VO2, postactivation potentiation).

warmup.q: How long should a warm-up last and how close to the activity should it finish?
warmup.a: Roughly 10-20 min, ending no more than ~15 min before the activity — after that the benefits start to dissipate.

warmup.q: Per the 5E, at what hold duration does static stretching clearly start to impair subsequent performance?
warmup.a: Beyond ~60 s; shorter holds (<45 s) cause only a small decrement, and short-duration stretching has no clear detriment under 60 s.

warmup.q: Why is the Potentiate phase most critical for a sprinter or a Hyrox athlete hitting fast stations?
warmup.a: The more speed/power an activity demands, the more the staged high-intensity efforts matter — treppe/PAPE make the muscle produce more force with less neural drive, so the athlete starts peaked rather than under-primed.

warmup.q: You want to prime a countermovement jump with a heavy back squat. Give a rough load, rep, and rest scheme.
warmup.a: ~60-90% 1RM, 1-3 sets of 1-3 reps, then rest ~3-12 min before the jump. Response is individual; weigh potentiation against fatigue.

warmup.q: Which PNF technique is most effective and why?
warmup.a: Hold-relax with agonist contraction — it recruits both reciprocal inhibition (agonist/hip-flexor contraction) and autogenic inhibition (hamstring contraction) to maximize ROM.

warmup.q: In PNF, how long and how hard is the isometric hold?
warmup.a: About 3-6 s at no more than 20% of maximum voluntary contraction; the first repetition usually gives the greatest benefit.

warmup.q: For long-term flexibility gains, does weekly frequency or single-session duration matter more?
warmup.a: Weekly stretch volume (frequency) appears more important than time per session — except PNF, where ~1 session/week may be optimal.

Both are active, but dynamic is controlled through a typically smaller ROM and avoids bouncing; ballistic uses intense bouncing to reach ROM, triggers the stretch reflex, and carries higher injury risk.

warmup.q: Why does slow static stretching not trigger the stretch reflex, but ballistic does?
warmup.a: Muscle spindles respond to rapid length change; slow static movement doesn't stimulate them, so the muscle relaxes and allows greater stretch, whereas rapid ballistic movement fires the spindle and reflexively contracts the muscle, limiting ROM.

warmup.q: Is there a strong link between stretching and injury prevention?
warmup.a: No consistent link. The chapter shifts warm-up focus toward performance; note both inflexibility and hyperflexibility can raise injury risk.

warmup.q: How does greater ROM potentially aid power output?
