warmup.a: Applying force over a greater ROM increases impulse by extending the time force is applied — but only if paired with the force capacity to control that range.

ex_squat.q: Name the two bar positions for the back squat and how each sits on the back.
ex_squat.a: Low bar: across the posterior deltoids at the middle of the trapezius, wider-than-shoulder grip. High bar: above the posterior deltoids at the base of the neck, grip only slightly wider than shoulders.

ex_squat.q: What are the three conditions that end the downward phase (any one of them)?
ex_squat.a: Tops of the thighs reach parallel to the floor, the trunk begins to round/flex forward, or the heels rise off the floor — stop at whichever occurs first.

ex_squat.q: List the 5E verbal cues for the back squat.
ex_squat.a: 'Chest up, head neutral'; 'Spread the floor with your feet'; 'Drive through your heels.'

ex_squat.q: How should hips and knees move during the ascent, and why?
ex_squat.a: Extend at the same rate so the torso-to-floor angle stays constant — this keeps the load balanced and prevents the hips shooting up ahead of the chest.

ex_squat.q: How is the back squat spotted, and how many spotters?
ex_squat.a: Two spotters, one at each end of the bar, in a shoulder-width stance with hands 2-3 in (5-8 cm) below the bar ends, thumbs crossed, moving sideways in unison with the athlete — never a single spotter directly behind.

ex_squat.q: A lifter's heels lift and the upper back rounds as they drive up. Name the errors and a cue.
ex_squat.a: Listed common errors: heels lifting off the floor and the upper back rounding (torso flexing further forward). Cue 'drive through your heels' and 'chest up, head neutral,' and reduce load until the pattern holds.

ex_squat.q: The knees cave inward at the bottom. Which joint action is this and what cue fixes it?
ex_squat.a: Knees moving in via hip adduction (valgus) — a listed error. Cue 'spread the floor with your feet' to keep knees aligned over the feet.

ex_squat.q: Why is the back squat classed as a structural exercise, and what does that imply for breathing?
ex_squat.a: It loads the vertebral column. Under heavy loads a well-trained lifter may use the Valsalva maneuver to support the spine, kept transient (~1-2 s) because blood pressure can rise to ~3× resting.

ex_squat.q: Default (non-Valsalva) breathing pattern for a resistance rep — when to exhale vs inhale?
ex_squat.a: Exhale through the sticking point of the concentric phase; inhale during the less stressful (eccentric) phase.

ex_squat.q: When is a weight belt indicated for squatting, and when is it unnecessary?
ex_squat.a: Indicated for lifts that stress the lower back during near-maximal or maximal loads; not needed for light-load squats or lifts that don't stress the low back. Overuse under-trains the abs and low back.

ex_squat.q: Why should power exercises (e.g., power clean, snatch) NOT be spotted?
ex_squat.a: The loads and bar speed make them too dangerous to spot; athletes are instead coached to safely miss/dump the bar rather than have a spotter catch it.

ex_squat.q: A lifter uses an open (false) grip on a heavy back squat. Why correct it?
ex_squat.a: A closed grip (thumb wrapped) is specified for the back squat; an open/false grip lets the bar roll or slip off the shelf, risking loss of control. Cue a closed, pronated grip and a high-elbow shelf.

ex_deadlift.q: Where does the bar start relative to the shins and feet in the conventional deadlift?
ex_deadlift.a: About 1 in (3 cm) in front of the shins, positioned over the balls of the feet.

ex_deadlift.q: Describe the set-up back and shoulder-blade position for the deadlift.
ex_deadlift.a: Back neutral or slightly arched, scapulae depressed and retracted, chest up and out, shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar, heels in contact with the floor.

ex_deadlift.q: When should a lifter switch from a pronated to an alternated grip on the deadlift?
ex_deadlift.a: When the load is too heavy to hold securely with a closed pronated (double-overhand) grip — the mixed grip stops the bar rolling out of the hands.

ex_deadlift.q: During the pull off the floor, what must happen to the torso-to-floor angle, and why?
ex_deadlift.a: It must stay constant — hips and shoulders rise together. If the hips rise first the angle changes and the lift becomes a stiff-legged good-morning, raising low-back load.

ex_deadlift.q: Name the prime movers of the conventional deadlift.
ex_deadlift.a: Gluteus maximus, the hamstrings (semimembranosus, semitendinosus, biceps femoris), and all four quadriceps (vastus lateralis, intermedius, medialis, rectus femoris).

ex_deadlift.q: List the two common errors the 5E flags for the conventional deadlift.
ex_deadlift.a: Letting the bar drift away from the body, and failing to lock out completely.

ex_deadlift.q: How is the stiff-leg deadlift's knee position different from the conventional pull, and which muscle joins the prime movers?
ex_deadlift.a: Knees are held in a fixed slight-to-moderate flexion throughout (no re-bend); the movement is a pure hip hinge. Erector spinae joins the glutes and hamstrings as a prime mover.

ex_deadlift.q: In the RDL, how far does the bar descend and where does it stay?
ex_deadlift.a: The bar slides down the thighs until it aligns with the patellar tendon and the torso is about parallel to the floor; it must maintain contact with the thighs the whole way.

ex_deadlift.q: An athlete's RDL keeps working the low back instead of the hamstrings. What set-up fault is likely, and how do you cue it?
ex_deadlift.a: Not pushing the hips back far enough, which reduces hamstring stretch. Cue 'push your hips back' and 'slide the bar down your thighs' with only a soft knee bend.

ex_deadlift.q: What turns a good morning into a squat, and how do you prevent it?
ex_deadlift.a: Flexing the knees too much. Keep the knees only softly flexed and hinge at the hips — buttocks travel straight back until the torso is ~parallel to the floor.

ex_deadlift.q: Why must the heels stay in contact with the floor in the stiff-leg deadlift?
ex_deadlift.a: Rising onto the toes shifts the weight forward off the mid-foot and encourages the back to round out of neutral — the bar should lower only until the plates touch while the back stays neutral and knees stay flexed.

ex_deadlift.q: What cue helps a lifter drive the deadlift without jerking the torso, and what does it target?
ex_deadlift.a: 'Push the floor away from you' — it emphasizes leg drive through the hips and knees so the bar breaks the floor without the low back yanking the load up.

ex_deadlift.q: At lockout of the deadlift, what is the finishing position and what should the lifter avoid?
ex_deadlift.a: Fully erect torso with hips and knees extended and glutes squeezed; avoid hyperextending (leaning back through) the lumbar spine.

ex_press.q: List the five points of the body contact position.
ex_press.a: Head, upper back/shoulders, and buttocks on the bench; right foot and left foot flat on the floor.

ex_press.q: What grip do you use for the barbell bench and shoulder press?
ex_press.a: A closed, pronated (overhand) grip, slightly wider than shoulder width.

ex_press.q: How low does the bar go in the bench press, and what path does it take up?
ex_press.a: Touch the chest at about nipple level, then push up and very slightly backward to full elbow extension.

ex_press.q: In the seated barbell shoulder press, where does the bar touch and what lets it get there?
ex_press.a: It lowers to touch the clavicles and anterior deltoids; the lifter extends the neck slightly so the bar passes by the face.

ex_press.q: Name three common errors in the barbell bench press.
ex_press.a: Bouncing the bar off the chest, lifting the hips off the bench, and not wrapping the thumbs around the bar (false grip).

ex_press.q: What grip does the spotter use on the bench press, and where are the hands placed?
ex_press.a: A closed, alternated grip, inside the athlete's hands.

ex_press.q: Why is the overhead/shoulder press more spotter- and lumbar-demanding than a lateral raise?
ex_press.a: The load travels over the face and overhead, which is harder to control and tempts low-back arching to finish; over-the-face and overhead barbell lifts warrant a spotter, unlike bar-at-the-sides raises.

ex_press.q: An athlete's hips pop off the bench at the top of a heavy bench rep. What error is this and why does it matter?
ex_press.a: Arching/lifting the hips off the bench — it breaks the five-point contact, shortens the ROM, and shifts load off the pecs while stressing the low back.

ex_press.q: During shoulder press the bar drifts backward and the low back over-arches. Give the fix.
ex_press.a: Cue 'bar path in front of the face' and 'push your head through at the top,' keep ribs down and glutes/abs braced, and don't rise off the seat — press vertical, not backward.

ex_press.q: Why is the push press NOT spotted, unlike the strict shoulder press?
ex_press.a: It's a power exercise — explosive hip/knee drive makes an assisted catch dangerous; the lifter is taught to bail (push the missed bar away) rather than be spotted.

ex_press.q: When spotting a dumbbell bench or shoulder press, where do you spot and why?
ex_press.a: At the forearms near the wrists, not the elbows/upper arms — if the elbows collapse, hands on the upper arm can't stop the dumbbells from striking the face or chest.

ex_press.q: How does the push press get the bar overhead, in two phases?
ex_press.a: A forceful hip and knee extension thrust drives the bar about one-half to two-thirds of the way up; from there the arms press it out to full elbow lockout while the hips and knees stay fully extended (no re-dip).

ex_press.q: What should the lifter do with breathing through the hardest part of a press rep?
ex_press.a: Exhale through the sticking point (early concentric) and inhale on the eccentric; experienced lifters may use a brief Valsalva on heavy structural lifts.

ex_press.q: Where does the spotter stand for a barbell bench press versus a seated shoulder press?
ex_press.a: Bench: erect and very close to the head of the bench. Shoulder press: erect behind the bench; both keep an alternated grip close to but not touching the bar as it moves.

ex_clean.q: Name the four phases of the power clean in order (5E).
ex_clean.a: First pull (floor→knee), transition (knee→power position), second pull (triple extension + shrug), catch (rack in a quarter-squat).

ex_clean.q: Which three joints extend in 'triple extension', and in which phase?
ex_clean.a: Hip, knee, and ankle — in the second pull.

ex_clean.q: Why must the first pull keep a constant torso-to-floor angle and not let the hips rise before the shoulders?
ex_clean.a: It preserves the back angle and keeps the bar close to the shins; if the hips shoot up, the bar drifts forward and the athlete has to jump forward to catch it.

ex_clean.q: What happens in the transition, and which everyday lift does it resemble?
ex_clean.a: As the bar clears the knees, the hips extend and the knees slide forward under the bar into the power position — the same pattern as the RDL.

ex_clean.q: Per 5E, when should the shoulder shrug happen relative to leg extension?
ex_clean.a: After the lower-body joints reach full extension — shrug vertically with the elbows still straight; bending the elbows early is a fault.

ex_clean.q: Describe the catch position of a power clean.
ex_clean.a: Bar racked across the clavicles/anterior delts in a quarter-squat, upper arms parallel to the floor (elbows high), torso near-erect with shoulders slightly ahead of hips, feet flat — then stand to finish.

ex_clean.q: 5E lists three common errors for the power clean — name them, and explain why the elbow-bend error kills power.
ex_clean.a: Letting the bar drift forward off the shins in the first pull, dropping the hips during the second pull, and flexing the elbows before the triple extension finishes. The early elbow bend turns an explosive leg-driven pull into a weak arm pull and cuts bar acceleration.

ex_clean.q: Why keep the heels on the floor as long as possible during the second pull?
ex_clean.a: To maximize force transfer into the bar; the heels only leave the floor at the very top of the extension.

ex_clean.q: What is the starting position of the clean-grip mid-thigh pull, and what joint angles define it?
ex_clean.a: The power position — knee ~125-145° and hip ~140-150°, trunk upright, shoulder over/slightly behind the bar.

ex_clean.q: A Hyrox athlete wants triple-extension power but has poor front-rack mobility. Which derivative fits and why?
ex_clean.a: The mid-thigh pull (or a high pull) — it trains the second pull's power without any catch or front-rack demand, and can be overloaded beyond clean 1RM.

ex_clean.q: How does a hang power clean differ from a floor power clean?
ex_clean.a: It begins from a hang (power position or just above/below the knee) and the bar is NOT returned to the floor between reps; it isolates the transition and second pull.

ex_clean.q: If a lifter jumps forward to catch every rep, which earlier fault is the likely cause?
ex_clean.a: The bar drifted forward during the first pull (center of pressure crept toward the toes), so the bar's path moved away from the shins.

ex_clean.q: Roughly what load can a trained athlete handle on a mid-thigh pull relative to their clean 1RM?
ex_clean.a: Up to about 140% of clean 1RM, because there is no catch to limit the load.

ex_clean.q: Where is the bar received relative to the thighs at the catch of a power clean (vs a full clean)?
ex_clean.a: With the top of the thighs above parallel (a partial/quarter squat) — a 'power' catch, not a deep front-squat receive.

ex_alt_modes.q: Is the kettlebell swing a squat or a hinge pattern?
ex_alt_modes.a: A hip hinge — explosive hip/knee extension and momentum float the bell to about eye level with a neutral back and passive arms; it is not a squat and is not arm-lifted.

ex_alt_modes.q: Why can the sled push be programmed at high frequency?
ex_alt_modes.a: It is nearly purely concentric, so it produces large metabolic/lactate stress but minimal muscle damage (creatine kinase stays flat) — recovery is fast, allowing daily or high-frequency use.

ex_alt_modes.q: What does the evidence say about kettlebells vs weightlifting for strength and jump?
ex_alt_modes.a: KBs suit general fitness but underperform weightlifting for max strength and jump: over 6 wk, weightlifting gave ~+4% vertical jump and +13.6% back squat vs kettlebell ~+0.8% and +4.5%.

ex_alt_modes.q: What sled loads and outcomes are supported for sprinting?
ex_alt_modes.a: Heavy resisted sleds improve acceleration (up to ~10 m) but not maximum-velocity sprinting; loads of 66-75% body mass have specifically produced a PAPE effect that cuts 15 m sprint times.

ex_alt_modes.q: Coaching points for a heavy sled push?
ex_alt_modes.a: Low near-horizontal trunk and shin angle, braced neutral spine, hands low on the posts, and short fast driving steps that lengthen as speed builds — keep the front foot behind the hip to apply horizontal force.

ex_alt_modes.q: Which strongman carry produces the greatest spinal compression, and how do you manage it?
ex_alt_modes.a: The yoke walk. Brace the spine hard (Valsalva as needed), keep it vertical, take short fast steps first, and use only loads you can control — it's rated among the most injury-prone lifts.

ex_alt_modes.q: Why is the tire flip a push, not a lift, and what injury does poor technique cause?
ex_alt_modes.a: Driving the tire forward with triple extension keeps momentum; 'muscling' it up as a lift slows it and overloads the biceps. The classic injury is a biceps tear when the hands slip on the tire.

ex_alt_modes.q: What core quality does a farmer's or suitcase carry train?
ex_alt_modes.a: Anti-lateral-flexion / anti-rotation trunk stability plus grip — resist any lateral lean (especially the single-arm suitcase carry), keep ribs stacked over pelvis and spine neutral-vertical.

ex_alt_modes.q: How should nontraditional implements be selected across athlete levels?
ex_alt_modes.a: Match the implement's demand profile to the sport, and reserve odd-object/strongman work for experienced athletes with sound technique; novices gain more from bodyweight, core, and basic ground-based lifts.

ex_alt_modes.q: You want a hard conditioning stimulus mid-week without adding soreness before a key session. Which implement fits and why?
ex_alt_modes.a: The sled — concentric-dominant, so it drives a big metabolic/lactate response with negligible muscle damage, giving conditioning stress that clears quickly and won't blunt the next session.

ex_alt_modes.q: Why does the log lift produce more impulse but less velocity/power than a barbell clean and jerk?
ex_alt_modes.a: The log's larger diameter makes it harder to move the body and implement quickly; it yields greater impulse and hip/trunk ROM but lower peak vertical velocity and power (a thinner log restores some power).

ex_alt_modes.q: What breathing/bracing applies to heavy structural carries and clean-type implement lifts?
ex_alt_modes.a: Loads that compress the axial skeleton warrant breath holding; above ~80% MVC or lighter loads to failure the Valsalva maneuver is often unavoidable and raises intra-abdominal pressure to stabilize the spine.

needs_analysis.q: What are the two stages of a needs analysis?
needs_analysis.a: Evaluation of the sport (movement, physiological, and injury analysis) and assessment of the athlete (training/injury status, technique experience, testing → primary goal).

needs_analysis.q: Where does needs analysis sit among the program-design variables?
needs_analysis.a: It is variable 1 of 7 and drives the other six: exercise selection, frequency, order, load/reps, volume, and rest.

needs_analysis.q: Name the three components of the sport evaluation.
needs_analysis.a: Movement analysis, physiological analysis (strength/power/hypertrophy/muscular-endurance priorities), and injury analysis (common sites + mechanisms, esp. non-contact).

needs_analysis.q: What four factors classify an athlete's resistance-training status (Table 18.1)?
needs_analysis.a: Training age, technique experience/skill, current program, and training stress — spanning beginner, intermediate, advanced on a continuum.

needs_analysis.q: Roughly how long training and what technique level defines an 'advanced' status?
needs_analysis.a: ≥1 year currently training, high training stress, high technique/skill.

needs_analysis.q: Why isn't 1RM testing sufficient on its own?
needs_analysis.a: It gives max dynamic load but no insight into rapid force production; add isometric (IMTP) and jump tests (CMJ slow SSC, rebound jump fast SSC).

needs_analysis.q: An athlete jumps 45 cm but has a 1.0 s time-to-takeoff and only average mRSI. What does the S&C coach conclude?
needs_analysis.a: Impulse is adequate but the force profile is poor (too slow) — emphasize maximal strength first, then speed-strength.

needs_analysis.q: Why cap hypertrophy for an endurance athlete but pursue it for a football lineman?
needs_analysis.a: Extra mass raises the carrying cost over long durations for endurance; for collision sports it protects against impact and ↑ momentum (mass × velocity) to win contacts.

needs_analysis.q: How many primary training goals per block, and how are other qualities kept?
needs_analysis.a: One primary goal per block via emphasis/de-emphasis; maintain other qualities with sufficient/retaining loads.

needs_analysis.q: What fraction of exercises is typically individualized between two athletes, and what is always individualized?
needs_analysis.a: Only ~10-20% of exercise selection differs (80-90% overlap), but the loads are always individualized to each athlete's capabilities.

needs_analysis.q: How would a needs analysis differ for a 16-yr developing soccer player vs a 35-yr recreational Hyrox athlete?
needs_analysis.a: Different sport demands (multidirectional speed/agility vs steady power-endurance), athlete maturity (LTAD/technique-building vs adult history), injury patterns, and goals.

needs_analysis.q: Which test isolates fast-SSC (reactive) function, and what index comes from it?
needs_analysis.a: The rebound jump; it yields RSI = rebound jump height ÷ ground-contact time.

needs_analysis.q: For a preseason (Table 18.2), what is the general resistance-training goal?
needs_analysis.a: Sport/movement-specific — strength, power, or muscular endurance depending on the sport — with medium sport-practice priority.

exercise_selection.q: What two criteria classify an exercise as core vs assistance?
exercise_selection.a: Size of muscle areas involved and number of primary joints: core = large muscle areas + ≥2 primary joints (multi-joint); assistance = small muscle area + single joint.

exercise_selection.q: Why do core exercises receive selection priority?
exercise_selection.a: They recruit large muscle areas across multiple joints and have greater dynamic correspondence — more direct transfer to athletic performance.

exercise_selection.q: How are the shoulder and the spine treated when classifying exercises?
exercise_selection.a: Each is counted as a single primary joint — so an overhead press's shoulder articulations count as one, and abdominal/back-extension lifts are single-joint assistance work.

exercise_selection.q: Define a structural exercise and give a direct- and indirect-loading example.
exercise_selection.a: A core lift that loads the spine and requires postural stabilization. Direct: back squat. Indirect: power clean.

exercise_selection.q: What distinguishes a power exercise from a structural exercise?
exercise_selection.a: A power exercise is a structural exercise performed very quickly/explosively to emphasize movement velocity and RFD.

exercise_selection.q: A coach adds leg extensions but no hamstring work, creating a quad-dominant program. What principle is violated and how is it fixed?
exercise_selection.a: Muscle balance — the agonist:antagonist ratio is off. Add hamstring exercises to restore proper strength balance across the knee.

exercise_selection.q: Does muscle balance mean opposing groups must be equally strong?
exercise_selection.a: No — it means a proper RATIO of strength/power/endurance between them, not equal strength.

exercise_selection.q: For a basketball jumper, why is the back squat preferred over the leg press despite both training hip/knee extensors?
exercise_selection.a: The squat is performed erect with balance and weight-bearing and achieves full hip extension, matching the jump — greater dynamic correspondence.

exercise_selection.q: State the recommended within-session exercise order and the rationale.
exercise_selection.a: Power → other core (most demanding first) → assistance. Power/core lifts have the highest skill and neuromuscular demand, are fatigue-prone, and earn the best strength adaptation when done fresh.

exercise_selection.q: If no power exercises are selected, what is the order?
exercise_selection.a: Core exercises first (most demanding first), then assistance exercises.

exercise_selection.q: For a weak, low-training-age athlete wanting more power, is ballistic training required?
exercise_selection.a: No — emphasizing maximal strength can raise power output as much as or more than ballistic work in weak/novice athletes.

exercise_selection.q: Give two practical constraints (besides sport demands) that shape exercise selection.
exercise_selection.a: Athlete's exercise-technique experience, available equipment, and time per session — e.g. leg press may replace lunge when time is short.

exercise_selection.q: How does a common use of assistance exercises relate to injury?
exercise_selection.a: They isolate specific muscles for injury prevention and rehab (e.g., shoulder external rotators for pitchers, quad reconditioning after contusion).

intensity_volume.q: State the goal rep range and %1RM for strength, hypertrophy, and muscular endurance (Table 18.11).
intensity_volume.a: Strength: ≤6 reps at ≥85% 1RM. Hypertrophy: 8-12 reps at 67-80%. Muscular endurance: ≥12 reps at ≤67%.

intensity_volume.q: Roughly how many reps can be performed at 85%, 75%, and 67% of 1RM (Table 18.8)?
What is the relationship between load and repetitions?
intensity_volume.a: Inverse — the heavier the load, the fewer reps you can complete. Choosing a goal automatically implies a load/rep pairing.

intensity_volume.q: Name the three ways to assign a training load (1RM methods).
intensity_volume.a: Directly test the 1RM; estimate 1RM from an xRM test (e.g. a 10RM); or test the goal-rep xRM directly (skips the % math).

intensity_volume.q: Define volume-load and how it differs from rep-volume.
intensity_volume.a: Volume-load = sets × reps × load (a proxy for mechanical work). Rep-volume = total number of reps performed, ignoring load.

intensity_volume.q: Calculate the volume-load for 4 sets of 6 reps of bench at 80 kg.
An athlete's bench 1RM is 100 kg. You want 4 reps for a strength day — what load?
A tested 10RM back squat is 300 lb. Estimate the 1RM.
State the 2-for-2 rule and one limitation.
intensity_volume.a: If the athlete beats the goal reps by 2+ on the last set in 2 consecutive workouts, add load. Limitation: it ignores technique/relative intensity and can drift athletes toward training to failure.

intensity_volume.q: Why cap assistance exercises at an 8RM load even in a heavy strength block?
intensity_volume.a: To minimize isolative stress on the single joint and connective tissue; core lifts can take heavier loads, assistance lifts should not go below ~8RM.

intensity_volume.q: The 5E says hypertrophy occurs across a wide load range. What's the practical takeaway for load choice?
intensity_volume.a: Both light (<60% to failure) and heavy (>60%) loads grow muscle similarly; heavier, lower-rep sets are more time-efficient and add strength, while low-load-to-failure carries more injury risk.

intensity_volume.q: Why do power exercises use lighter loads than the %1RM-rep table would predict for their rep count?
intensity_volume.a: Movement quality/velocity degrades before true momentary failure, so power is trained with submaximal loads (~75-90% for weightlifting derivatives, up to 30% for ballistic throws) at ≤5 fast reps — not true RMs.

intensity_volume.q: You're programming a Hyrox athlete's off-season strength phase. Sets and reps for the main lifts?
intensity_volume.a: Strength goal (trained): ≤6 goal reps, 2-3 sets at ≥85% 1RM on core lifts (Table 18.13).

intensity_volume.q: How should RIR/RPE be prescribed and why pair it with %1RM?
intensity_volume.a: Prescribe as a range (RIR 2-3, RPE 7-8). Pair with %1RM so a baseline load is set objectively, then autoregulate up/down by feel — RIR accuracy drops the further from failure.

rest_frequency.q: Rest interval for strength vs hypertrophy vs muscular endurance.
rest_frequency.a: Strength 2-5 min; hypertrophy 2-3 min for multijoint (60-90 s single-joint); muscular endurance ≤30 s.

rest_frequency.q: A bench-press strength day includes a 12RM lateral raise. What rest does the raise get, and why?
rest_frequency.a: ~1 min. Rest is assigned by the RELATIVE load of each exercise, not the program's overall goal — the light 12RM assistance load recovers fast.
Beginner 2-3, intermediate 3-4, advanced 4-7 (advanced may include multiple sessions/day).

rest_frequency.q: Frequency guidelines across the sport season.
At equated weekly volume-load, does training a muscle more often produce more strength or hypertrophy?
rest_frequency.a: No — adaptations are comparable regardless of frequency (and of split vs total-body) once weekly volume-load is equated.

rest_frequency.q: How many rest days between sessions that stress the same muscle groups?
rest_frequency.a: At least 1 but not more than 3; too few impairs recovery, while too many (e.g. no stimulus from Wed to the next Mon) can cost training status.

rest_frequency.q: Why are power/weightlifting exercises programmed first in a session?
rest_frequency.a: High neuromuscular/skill demand and fast fatigue — done fresh to protect technique, reduce injury risk, and preserve force output.

rest_frequency.q: General exercise-order sequence when power lifts are included.
rest_frequency.a: Power → other (non-power) core exercises → assistance exercises; equivalently large-muscle/multijoint before small-muscle/single-joint.

rest_frequency.q: Difference between a superset and a compound set.
rest_frequency.a: Superset = two exercises for OPPOSING muscles (agonist/antagonist) back-to-back; compound set = two exercises for the SAME muscle group back-to-back.

rest_frequency.q: Why alternate upper- and lower-body exercises when time is limited?
rest_frequency.a: One region rests while the other works, cutting inter-exercise rest and total session time; helpful for novices who can't string same-region lifts.

rest_frequency.q: In a lower/upper 4-day split, why can Mon-Tue back-to-back days still respect recovery?
rest_frequency.a: Lower Mon/Thu and upper Tue/Fri gives 2-3 days between sessions for the SAME region, even though the athlete trains on consecutive calendar days.

rest_frequency.q: You have 3 pulling exercises (pull-up, row, hammer curl) in a row. What's the problem and the fix?
rest_frequency.a: Shared biceps fatigues, cutting reps on later lifts. Fix: alternate push/pull so no muscle is hit in consecutive exercises.

rest_frequency.q: What is microdosing and when is it useful?
rest_frequency.a: Splitting weekly volume across multiple short sessions; useful in-season or when competing time demands make long high-volume sessions too fatiguing.

rest_frequency.q: Which recovers faster from heavy loading: upper or lower body? Single- or multijoint?
rest_frequency.a: Upper body recovers faster than lower; single-joint recovers faster than multijoint — hence e.g. only one very heavy squat/deadlift day/wk.

plyometrics.q: Name the three phases of the SSC in order and the key events in each.
plyometrics.a: Eccentric (agonist stretch, SEC stores elastic energy, spindles stimulated) → amortization (Ia afferents synapse alpha motor neurons; keep short) → concentric (elastic energy released, alpha motor neurons drive reflexive shortening).

plyometrics.q: Why must the amortization phase stay short?
plyometrics.a: A long delay lets stored elastic energy dissipate as heat and prevents the stretch reflex from potentiating the concentric action—erasing the plyometric benefit.

plyometrics.q: Distinguish the mechanical vs neurophysiological model of plyometrics.
plyometrics.a: Mechanical: rapid stretch loads the SEC (mostly tendon), storing/returning elastic energy. Neurophysiological: rapid stretch fires muscle spindles → stretch reflex potentiates the concentric contraction.

plyometrics.q: Ballistic vs rebound drills—define and give examples.
plyometrics.a: Ballistic minimally uses the neuro SSC (squat jump, MB overhead throw). Rebound requires physiomechanical SSC (depth/drop jumps, repeat CMJs) and yields higher muscle activation, power, and impulse.

plyometrics.q: How is lower-body plyometric volume measured, and what per-session ranges apply by experience?
plyometrics.a: In foot contacts per session: beginner 80-100, intermediate 100-120, advanced 120-200—lower within the range as drill intensity rises.

plyometrics.q: A beginner does depth jumps off a 12-in box for 3 sets of 8. Contacts per session? Is the volume appropriate?
plyometrics.a: 8 landings × 3 sets = 24 foot contacts. Well under the beginner 80-100 range, but depth jumps are high-intensity—for a true beginner, depth jumps are typically not recommended; regress to in-place jumps/low hops first.

plyometrics.q: What is the fast-vs-slow SSC cutoff and why does it matter?
plyometrics.a: Ground-contact time <0.25 s = fast SSC, >0.25 s = slow SSC (Schmidtbleicher). Reactive/rebound work trains the fast SSC most relevant to sprinting and reactive jumps.

plyometrics.q: Give the squat-strength guidelines tied to plyometric intensity.
plyometrics.a: Repeated hops/jumps ~1× BW back squat; bounding and depth jumps off ≤12-in box ~1.5× BW; depth/box jumps off >12-in box ~2× BW. RFD matters as much as peak strength.

plyometrics.q: Recommended recovery between plyometric sessions and typical weekly frequency?
plyometrics.a: 48-72 h between sessions (power/DOMS can persist up to 72 h), giving ~2-3 sessions/week; overall range 1-4×/wk by sport, status, and season.

plyometrics.q: What surfaces are appropriate for landing, and which extend amortization?
plyometrics.a: Grass, suspended floor, or rubber mat. Concrete/tile/hardwood are unsafe (no shock absorption). Very thick mats (≥6 in) and mini-trampolines extend the amortization phase and blunt the stretch reflex.

plyometrics.q: Describe proper landing technique and the 'credit-card' cue.
plyometrics.a: Shoulders over knees over toes, knees tracking over toes (avoid dynamic valgus). Dorsiflex approaching contact, then aggressively plantarflex to attack the ground—land midfoot/forefoot with heels off (a credit card should slide under the heel), raising SSC efficacy and RFD.

plyometrics.q: Why sequence plyometrics before aerobic endurance work in a mixed session?
plyometrics.a: Aerobic training interferes with power production, so doing plyos first preserves the quality and output of the explosive work.

plyometrics.q: Which population has depth jumps contraindicated, and why?
plyometrics.a: Prepubescent children—their epiphyseal (growth) plates are still open, so high-intensity depth jumps and similar drills are contraindicated.

plyometrics.q: For a 100+ kg (>220 lb) athlete, what depth-jump box constraint applies?
plyometrics.a: Approach high-volume/high-intensity plyos with caution and cap depth-jump box height at ≤18 in (46 cm); greater mass raises joint compressive forces and injury risk.

speed_agility.q: Define speed, change of direction, and agility as the 5E separates them.
speed_agility.a: Speed = skills/abilities to reach high velocity. COD = pre-planned deceleration, COM deflection, then reacceleration (closed loop). Agility = change of direction/velocity/mode in response to a stimulus, adding perceptual-cognitive demand.

speed_agility.q: Why is RFD more important than maximal strength for sprint and COD actions?
speed_agility.a: Maximal voluntary force takes ~300 ms+ to develop, but sprint and COD ground contacts last only 0-200 ms — the athlete never has time to express max force, so the rate of force production and net impulse limit performance.

speed_agility.q: What two variables determine sprint speed, and what underlies both?
speed_agility.a: Step length × step frequency. Both are driven by rapid, high force application into the ground — chiefly vertical GRF in a short stance phase.

speed_agility.q: Contrast acceleration mechanics with maximum-velocity mechanics.
speed_agility.a: Acceleration: big forward lean, COM ahead of foot, long piston-like contacts, forward shin angles, gradually rising hips. Max-velocity: tall upright posture, high knee/front-side mechanics, brief cyclic contacts, foot striking down-and-back close under the COM.

speed_agility.q: How does cutting angle change the physical demand of a COD, and what quality do you emphasize for sharp cuts?
speed_agility.a: As angle increases (≥75°), ground contact time rises above ~250 ms and braking/negative work climbs. Emphasize eccentric strength for braking plus maximal strength, with reactive strength for the eccentric-concentric coupling.

speed_agility.q: What is the COD deficit and why use it?
speed_agility.a: COD-test time minus a linear sprint time of equal length (e.g., 5-0-5 time − 10 m sprint time). It strips out linear-speed bias so you measure the athlete's true change-of-direction ability, not just how fast they run.

speed_agility.q: How is agility developed differently from COD in a periodized plan?
speed_agility.a: Start with closed-skill preplanned COD drills, progress physical loading, then layer a generic stimulus (whistle, arrow, light), and finally sport-specific stimuli via small-sided/evasion games. Random small-sided games alone are less effective.

speed_agility.q: Define impulse and state its relationship to momentum.
speed_agility.a: Impulse = force × the time it acts (area under the force-time curve). Net impulse equals the change in momentum; since mass is constant, greater net impulse means greater change in velocity.

speed_agility.q: Why does the spring-mass model describe recreational athletes better than elite sprinters?
speed_agility.a: Non-elite produce a symmetrical vertical-force curve matching the compress-then-extend spring. Elite sprinters front-load most vertical force into the first half of stance, deviating from the classic symmetrical model.

speed_agility.q: An athlete overstrides at top speed. Why is this counterproductive, per 5E?
speed_agility.a: Reaching for longer steps lengthens ground contact, damps the stretch-shortening cycle, lowers step frequency, and misdirects force (more braking) — plus raises hamstring injury risk. Speed is limited by vertical force application, not by reaching farther.

speed_agility.q: A 180° cut (5-0-5) has entry velocity ~5 m/s and the athlete masses 80 kg. What is sprint momentum, and why track it?
speed_agility.a: Momentum = mass × velocity = 80 × 5 = 400 kg·m/s. Higher momentum means a larger braking impulse is needed to decelerate, so it indexes the braking demand of the COD.

speed_agility.q: Name the perceptual-cognitive components that make agility a distinct training quality.
speed_agility.a: Visual search/scanning, anticipation, pattern recognition, knowledge of the situation, decision-making time and accuracy, and reaction time — plus tactical context (offensive vs defensive).

speed_agility.q: What eccentric-focused exercises does 5E recommend to build COD braking capacity?
speed_agility.a: Drop landings, landing from a loaded jump, the catch phase of a power clean/snatch, flywheel training, accentuated/supramaximal eccentrics, and tempo work — trained at both low and high eccentric velocities.

speed_agility.q: What work:rest logic applies to speed/agility drills, and how long are agility sessions in the 5E example?
speed_agility.a: Keep bouts brief with frequent rest to protect quality and technique (SSC/RFD are CNS-limited). The 5E agility block runs 10-15 min per session inclusive of work and rest, 1-3×/week.

aerobic_programming.q: List the 5 aerobic program design variables in the order 5E prescribes them.
aerobic_programming.a: 1) Exercise mode, 2) training frequency, 3) training intensity, 4) exercise duration, 5) exercise progression.

aerobic_programming.q: 5E renames 'long slow distance'. What term does it prefer, and what are the intensity/duration prescriptions?
aerobic_programming.a: Extensive endurance training. Intensity ≤65% VO2max (zone 2, conversational); duration ~30 min to 2 h, distance ≥ race distance where practical.

aerobic_programming.q: What intensity anchors pace/tempo training, and what are the two ways to run it?
aerobic_programming.a: At the maximal lactate steady state (MLSS). Steady (continuous ~20–30 min) or intermittent (cruise/tempo intervals with brief recoveries).

aerobic_programming.q: Classic interval training: intensity, work bout length, and work:rest ratio?
aerobic_programming.a: Near VO2max; work bouts 3–5 min (as short as 30 s); W:R = 1:1 (rest equals work for 3–5 min bouts).

aerobic_programming.q: Describe the Tabata HIIT protocol and how much time above 90% VO2max HIIT should accumulate.
aerobic_programming.a: 8 rounds of 20 s hard / 10 s rest, hard enough to exhaust by rep 7–8. Buchheit & Laursen: accumulate several minutes >90% VO2max per session for optimal stimulus.

aerobic_programming.q: What is fartlek and roughly what intensity range does it span?
aerobic_programming.a: Swedish 'speed play' — unstructured ~20–60 min session mixing easy (~70% VO2max) with faster hill/burst efforts (~85–90% VO2max), blending LSD, tempo, and interval work.

aerobic_programming.q: The 10% progression rule — what does it govern and what's the key caveat for beginners?
aerobic_programming.a: Increase frequency, intensity, OR duration by no more than ~10%/wk, and only one variable at a time. Beginners starting from a very low base can safely exceed 10%.

aerobic_programming.q: Why does 5E recommend setting session progression by time rather than distance?
aerobic_programming.a: Time accounts for terrain and environmental conditions (hills, heat) that raise the true stress of a fixed distance; distance does not, so equal distance can mean very different loads.

aerobic_programming.q: Karvonen: a 40-yr-old with RHR 55 is prescribed 70% intensity. Compute target HR.
%MHR method: 25-yr-old prescribed 85% of max HR. Target HR?
Match session type to primary adaptation: extensive endurance, pace/tempo, interval.
aerobic_programming.a: Extensive endurance → mitochondrial density, fat use, LT (base); pace/tempo → economy, LT, MLSS (race-pace); interval → VO2max + anaerobic metabolism.

aerobic_programming.q: Polarized (Seiler) distribution and its measurement basis — how does it map onto 5E's zones?
aerobic_programming.a: ~80% of SESSIONS easy (below LT1, ≈ zone 1–2) and ~20% hard (above LT2, ≈ zone 4–5), minimizing the middle gray zone. Measured by session count, not minute share. It refines 5E's intensity-zone framework.

aerobic_programming.q: Tapering: typical duration and what you cut vs. hold?
aerobic_programming.a: 7–28 days. Cut training VOLUME while maintaining intensity and frequency (linear, step, or progressive models) to restore freshness for peak performance.

aerobic_programming.q: Concurrent strength for an endurance athlete — how do you dose it to protect the run?
aerobic_programming.a: Heavy or plyometric strength 2–3×/wk; keep loads high (≥~90% 1RM / ≤4RM) but weekly volume low to limit fatigue carryover. Maximal strength beats submaximal for economy gains; strength holds up to ~12 wks on 1 session/wk.

periodization_models.q: Distinguish the parallel, sequential, and emphasis planning models and who each suits.
periodization_models.a: Parallel: all qualities at once — novices/youth. Sequential: one focused block at a time, residual effects carry forward — advanced athletes, individual sports. Emphasis: concurrent training with rotating primary focus (~2 wk) and others at maintenance — congested schedules.

periodization_models.q: Why does 5E call 'linear periodization' a misnomer?
periodization_models.a: A central tenet of periodization is REMOVING linearity. The traditional Matveyev/Stone model only looks linear at the macro scale; at micro/meso level volume-load undulates (heavy/light days, unloading weeks), so it is actually nonlinear.

periodization_models.q: State the three mechanistic theories underpinning periodization.
periodization_models.a: General adaptation syndrome (GAS), stimulus-fatigue-recovery-adaptation theory, and the fitness-fatigue paradigm.

periodization_models.q: In the fitness-fatigue paradigm, why does a taper raise performance?
periodization_models.a: Preparedness = fitness − fatigue. Fatigue dissipates ~2/3 faster than fitness, so cutting load sheds fatigue quickly while fitness lingers → net preparedness rises.

periodization_models.q: List the traditional resistance-training phase sequence across a macrocycle.
periodization_models.a: Hypertrophy/strength-endurance → basic strength → strength/power (first transition) → peaking or maintenance (competitive) → active rest (second transition).

periodization_models.q: Give the intensity/volume prescription for the hypertrophy vs basic-strength phase.
periodization_models.a: Hypertrophy/strength-endurance: 50-75% 1RM, 3-6 sets × 8-20 reps, high volume. Basic strength: 80-95% 1RM, 2-6 sets × 2-6 reps, moderate-high.

periodization_models.q: How long can peak performance be held, and what limits maintenance duration?
periodization_models.a: ~7-14 days. The duration it can be maintained is inversely related to the average intensity of the training plan.

periodization_models.q: Name the periodization cycles from longest to shortest with typical durations.
periodization_models.a: Multiyear (2-4 yr) → annual (1 yr) → macrocycle (months-year) → mesocycle (2-6 wk, 4 typical) → microcycle (days-2 wk, ~1 wk typical) → training day → session.

periodization_models.q: A trained lifter runs Tue 4×6RM, Thu 3×10RM, Sat 5×3RM. Which model, and what varies?
periodization_models.a: Daily undulating periodization (DUP). Load, volume/reps, and training focus (strength/hypertrophy/power) all vary within the same microcycle.

periodization_models.q: Why should the second transition (active rest) not exceed ~4 weeks?
periodization_models.a: Prolonged reduced training causes detraining/loss of performance capacity, forcing a much longer preparatory period to regain form.

periodization_models.q: You have 16 wk off-season before a Hyrox race. Sketch the periodized structure.
periodization_models.a: General prep (hypertrophy/strength-endurance base + aerobic volume) → specific prep (basic strength + threshold/race-modality work) → first transition (strength/power + race-pace compromise runs) → peak/taper 1-2 wk into race. Insert an unloading microcycle at each meso's end.

periodization_models.q: For an in-season team athlete, what maintenance loading keeps strength while managing fatigue?
periodization_models.a: Moderate-to-high intensity (~85-93% 1RM) at low-to-moderate volume (~2-5 sets × 3-6 reps), modulated at the microcycle level around games and travel.

periodization_models.q: What does the GAS predict if a heavy DUP week stacks too much volume-load in an elite athlete?
periodization_models.a: Accumulated fatigue can push toward the exhaustion phase → reduced preparedness and increased overtraining/injury risk; 5E flags this as a DUP caution for high-level athletes.

periodization_structure.q: State the typical durations of macro-, meso-, and microcycles.
periodization_structure.a: Macro: several months to a year. Meso: 2–6 weeks (4 wk most common). Micro: several days to 2 weeks (1 wk / 7 days most common).
Periodization is macro-management — the annual/multiyear scaffold of periods and phases. Programming is micro-management — the actual modes, methods, loads, sets/reps that fill that scaffold.

periodization_structure.q: Name the four periods of the traditional model in order and their strength focus.
periodization_structure.a: Preparatory (hypertrophy/strength-endurance → basic strength), first transition (strength/power), competitive (peaking or maintenance), second transition (active rest).

periodization_structure.q: How long can peak performance realistically be held, and what shortens it?
periodization_structure.a: ~7–14 days. Maintainable duration is inversely related to the average training intensity of the plan.

periodization_structure.q: Why does reducing training load before competition raise preparedness?
periodization_structure.a: Fatigue dissipates ~2/3 faster than fitness, so cutting load sheds fatigue while fitness lingers — the fitness–fatigue summation nets higher preparedness.

periodization_structure.q: Give the intensity/volume prescription for the hypertrophy vs basic strength phases.
periodization_structure.a: Hypertrophy/strength-endurance: 50–75% 1RM, 3–6 sets × 8–20 reps (hi vol, lo–mod int). Basic strength: 80–95% 1RM, 2–6 sets × 2–6 reps (mod–hi vol, ↑int).

periodization_structure.q: Name the three GAS phases and what each represents.
periodization_structure.a: Alarm (fatigue/soreness, performance dips), resistance (adapts, returns to/above baseline = supercompensation), exhaustion (stress persists → overreaching/overtraining).

periodization_structure.q: A sprinter is in specific preparatory phase. What shifts vs general prep?
periodization_structure.a: Training becomes more sport-specific — interval sprints at moderate distance, specialized plyos, sport-similar lifts (squat, power clean); loads rise toward basic strength (80–95% 1RM, lower reps).

periodization_structure.q: Why is 'linear periodization' considered a false label for the traditional model?
periodization_structure.a: A core tenet of periodization is removing linearity. Even holding sets/reps and only raising %1RM, volume-load undulates at the micro/meso level, so loading is nonlinear.

periodization_structure.q: How long should the second transition (active rest) last and why the cap?
periodization_structure.a: No longer than ~1–4 weeks. Prolonged reduced training causes detraining, forcing a longer general preparatory phase to regain form.

periodization_structure.q: Which planning model suits a novice/youth athlete, and which suits an advanced athlete with a congested schedule?
periodization_structure.a: Parallel (concurrent) model for novices/developmental athletes; sequential or emphasis models for intermediate-to-elite athletes needing greater variation/stimulus.

periodization_structure.q: Contrast daily undulating (DUP) with the traditional model within a microcycle.
periodization_structure.a: DUP changes load, volume, AND focus across days (e.g., 6RM strength / 10RM hypertrophy / 3RM power). Traditional keeps sets/reps fixed and varies only load (heavy→light), though volume-load still undulates.

periodization_structure.q: Off-season basketball player follows 4 wk mesos. Where do you place the unload, and why?
periodization_structure.a: In the last microcycle of each meso. It reduces accumulated fatigue so fitness supercompensates before the next phase/key contest.

periodization_structure.q: A 100 m sprinter runs 400 m reps early in the plan. Which phase, and what's the rationale?
periodization_structure.a: General preparatory (hypertrophy/strength-endurance). Longer-than-race efforts at slower speed build the aerobic/work-capacity base that later high-intensity sport-specific work is built on.

rehab.q: Name the three tissue-healing phases in order with rough timelines.
rehab.a: Inflammation (~72h) → fibroblastic repair (~72h to ~8 wk) → maturation-remodeling (weeks to years). Phases overlap.

rehab.q: How does macrotrauma differ from microtrauma? Give an example of each.
rehab.a: Macrotrauma = single sudden overload (e.g. ankle sprain, fracture). Microtrauma = repeated overuse with too little recovery (e.g. stress fracture, tendinopathy).

rehab.q: Is a 'sprain' a ligament or muscle injury? What about a 'strain'?
rehab.a: Sprain = ligament injury; strain = muscle/tendon injury. Both graded 1st/2nd/3rd degree.

rehab.q: What does 5E's POLICE stand for, and what did it replace?
rehab.a: Protection, Optimal Loading, Compression, Ice, Elevation (the 5E's ordering) — replaced PRICE by adding early optimal loading for faster recovery.

rehab.q: Which collagen type dominates in the repair phase vs remodeling, and why does it matter?
rehab.a: Repair lays weak, randomly-oriented type-III collagen; remodeling converts it to strong type-I collagen aligned to lines of stress — hence tissue is fragile early and strengthens with progressive loading.

rehab.q: During inflammation, which cells release the growth factors that trigger the repair phase?
rehab.a: Macrophages and platelets in the injured area release growth factors.

rehab.q: Why does eccentric loading specifically aid tissue repair?
rehab.a: It drives mechanotransduction — mechanical overload signals the cell to upregulate DNA and ↑collagen synthesis; eccentrics also produce high force at low energy cost and cut hamstring-strain risk.

rehab.q: Contrast CKC and OKC with an example of each. Which is generally more 'functional'?
rehab.a: CKC = distal segment fixed (squat) — multi-joint, more joint stability, more sport-functional. OKC = terminal joint free (leg extension) — isolates a single joint/muscle.

rehab.q: A limitation of isometric strengthening in rehab, and the fix?
rehab.a: Strength gains are joint-angle specific (only at trained angles) — so train isometrically at multiple angles.

rehab.q: What RTS criteria beyond 'pain-free' should the CSCS see before clearing an athlete?
rehab.a: Side-to-side deficit <10% (LSI ≥90%) in strength/function, restored ROM, sport-specific functional testing, and patient-reported outcomes — pain resolves before tissue fully heals.

rehab.q: A soccer player has an acute right-knee injury. What can the CSCS still train in the inflammatory phase?
rehab.a: Uninjured regions — upper body, cardiorespiratory fitness, and the uninvolved left leg (e.g. single-leg squat); training the healthy limb can even improve strength in the injured one (cross-education). No active loading of the injured knee.

rehab.q: In DAPRE, an athlete does 5 reps on set 3 (100% of 6RM). What happens next?
rehab.a: 5-7 reps on set 3 → keep the same resistance for set 4, then add 5-10 lb (2.3-4.5 kg) for the next session.

rehab.q: Contrast DeLorme vs Oxford resistance progressions.
rehab.a: Both 3×10 off the 10RM. DeLorme goes light→heavy (50%, 75%, 100% of 10RM — built-in warm-up). Oxford reverses it heavy→light (100%, 75%, 50%) so it needs a separate non-fatiguing warm-up set.

rehab.q: What is arthrogenic inhibition and why does it matter in rehab?
rehab.a: Joint swelling reflexively inhibits the alpha motor neuron, causing muscle weakness/atrophy — so controlling edema early is key to restoring activation.

facility_design.q: Minimum floor space recommended per person in an S&C facility?
facility_design.a: 100 sq ft (9.3 m²) per person, counting athletes plus all staff present.

facility_design.q: List the four phases of new facility design in order.
What three deliverables make up the predesign phase (before hiring the architect)?
facility_design.a: Needs analysis, feasibility (SWOT) study, and master plan.

facility_design.q: Recommended lighting for general gym areas vs. dedicated resistance/group areas?
facility_design.a: 20-30 fc (200-300 lux) general; up to 50 fc (500 lux) for lifting/group exercise.

facility_design.q: Recommended width for main walkways in the weight room?
Minimum spacing on each side of a power rack / between racks, barbells, dumbbells?
facility_design.a: 3 ft (0.9 m) minimum; add more if spotters are needed.

facility_design.q: Optimal temperature and max relative humidity for an S&C facility?
facility_design.a: Optimal 68-74°F (broader OK 68-78°F); relative humidity should not exceed 60%.

facility_design.q: Mirror placement rules and the reason for the height rule?
facility_design.a: ≥6 in from equipment, ≥20 in above floor — so dropped/rolling weights (18-in plate) don't hit and shatter the glass.

facility_design.q: Recommended ceiling height, and why higher may be needed?
facility_design.a: 12-14 ft (3.7-4.3 m); more for overhead throws, box/vertical jumps, and weightlifting clearance.

facility_design.q: How often should room air be exchanged?
facility_design.a: Minimum 8-10 times/hour, optimally 12-15 times/hour to avoid odors and stagnant air.

facility_design.q: Coach runs two groups simultaneously in one room — how should equipment be arranged for supervision and flow?
facility_design.a: Group by movement pattern, racks/free weights along walls, shorter equipment mid-room for clear line of sight, machines back-to-back in rows creating 5-7 ft walkways; divide into sections to limit overlap.

facility_design.q: You bolt a suspension trainer for a crew circuit — anchor height and connector rating?
facility_design.a: Anchor 7-9 ft above floor; use fall-rated carabiners ≥5000 lb (22 kN, OSHA) with a secondary screw-lock; never mount in/near doorways.

facility_design.q: Calc: floor space for a back squat from a rack using a 7-ft Olympic bar (depth = 10 ft user space, width = bar + 6 ft double-wide cushion).
Calc: bench press space with a 6-ft bench and 7-ft bar. Depth = bench + 3 ft cushion; width = bar + 6 ft double-wide cushion.

facility_legal.q: Name the four elements that must all be present to establish negligence.
facility_legal.a: Duty, breach of duty, proximate cause, and damages.

facility_legal.q: What burden of proof applies to each element of negligence in a civil case?
facility_legal.a: Preponderance of the evidence — the greater weight of evidence.

facility_legal.q: How is standard of care defined for an S&C professional?
facility_legal.a: What a reasonable, prudent person with the same education, training, and certification (e.g., CSCS, CPR, AED, first aid) would do in similar circumstances.

facility_legal.q: Does a signed assumption-of-risk waiver protect you if an athlete is injured?
facility_legal.a: No — it may not shield you from liability if you are found negligent when the damage occurs.

facility_legal.q: Who is legally permitted to medically clear an athlete to begin an S&C program?
facility_legal.a: Only sports-medicine staff (certified athletic trainer, physician, or physical therapist) — clearing/diagnosing is outside the S&C pro's scope of practice.

facility_legal.q: What are the NSCA professional-to-participant supervision ratios by level?
What minimum floor space per participant does the NSCA recommend?
facility_legal.a: At least 100 ft² of average floor space per participant.

facility_legal.q: In roughly what fraction of athletic-injury court cases was supervision cited?
facility_legal.a: About 80% — inadequate instruction/supervision is a leading cause of litigation.

facility_legal.q: An athlete with sickle cell trait is run through the same work-to-rest ratios as everyone else and collapses. Why is the S&C pro exposed to negligence?
facility_legal.a: They had a duty to modify the work-to-rest ratio for a known foreseeable-risk condition, breached it, and that breach proximately caused foreseeable harm — all four negligence elements are met.

facility_legal.q: List the core components of an emergency action plan.
facility_legal.a: EMS activation procedure, contact names/numbers, facility address, phone and exit locations, designated qualified responders, AED/first-aid kit location, ambulance access, and action plans for fire/injury/environmental threats.

facility_legal.q: How often must the EAP be practiced, and what certifications must staff hold?
facility_legal.a: Practiced at least quarterly; staff maintain current standard first aid, CPR, and AED certification.

facility_legal.q: Why is using exercise or conditioning as punishment prohibited?
facility_legal.a: It carries negligent/reckless-conduct exposure (hazing, mental abuse) and adds physical burden that raises injury/sudden-death risk — best practice bars any added burden.

facility_legal.q: How long should injury reports and related records be kept, and why?
facility_legal.a: Indefinitely — the statute of limitations for filing suit varies by jurisdiction, so records must outlast any possible claim.

facility_legal.q: What single credential establishes a standard of care, and which body mandated it for NCAA Division I in 2015?
facility_legal.a: The CSCS (or equivalent accredited certification); NCAA Division I adopted legislation requiring current accredited S&C certification effective Aug 1, 2015.

phase1_review.q: Name the three determinants of whole-muscle force output integrated across Ch 1-2.
phase1_review.a: Muscle cross-sectional area (architecture), neural drive (recruitment per the size principle + rate coding), and mechanical leverage (moment arms).

phase1_review.q: Order the three energy systems by ATP production RATE (fastest first) and by CAPACITY (largest first).
phase1_review.a: Rate: phosphagen > fast glycolysis > oxidative. Capacity: oxidative > fast glycolysis > phosphagen.

phase1_review.q: In anaerobic training, what adapts first and when does the balance shift?
phase1_review.a: Neural adaptations dominate early (~6-10 wk, Sale); after >10 wk hypertrophy contributes more to strength/power gains.

phase1_review.q: What is the single most significant cardiovascular change with long-term aerobic training, and what drives it?
phase1_review.a: Increased maximal cardiac output, driven primarily by increased stroke volume; maximal heart rate stays roughly unchanged.

phase1_review.q: Why is the elbow (biceps curl) a third-class lever, and what does its MA<1 trade off?
phase1_review.a: Muscle force and resistive force are on the same side of the fulcrum with the muscle force between axis and load. MA<1 means the muscle out-forces the load, trading force for greater range of motion and end-point speed.

phase1_review.q: Contrast the anabolic and catabolic hormones and their net role in adaptation.
phase1_review.a: Anabolic: testosterone, GH, IGF-I (build/repair tissue). Catabolic: cortisol (mobilizes substrate, degrades protein when chronically elevated). Adaptation depends on keeping net signaling anabolic within recovery.

phase1_review.q: During eccentric loading (lowering the sled/lift), how does force capacity compare to concentric?
phase1_review.a: Eccentric force capacity exceeds isometric, which exceeds concentric at a given velocity — the muscle can resist more than it can lift.

phase1_review.q: Write the Fick relationship for VO2max and name the aerobic-training adaptation on each side.
phase1_review.a: VO2max = max cardiac output × max a-vO2 difference. Left side improves centrally (↑SV→↑Q); right side improves peripherally (↑mitochondria, capillary density → wider a-vO2 diff).

phase1_review.q: Applied: for a 4×8 interval at HR 170-175 (above lactate threshold), walk one chain from neural recruitment to logged adaptation.
phase1_review.a: High force/velocity → size principle recruits Type II motor units → fast glycolysis supplies ATP with lactate accumulation → HR near max, SV plateaued, Q maximized → catecholamine/cortisol/GH response → drives mitochondrial biogenesis, lactate-clearance enzymes, capillary density.

phase1_review.q: Why does CP replenish ATP faster than glycolysis, and what limits the phosphagen system?
phase1_review.a: CP resynthesizes ATP in a single creatine-kinase step (no multi-enzyme chain), so its rate is highest; but CP stores are small (~4-6× resting ATP), so capacity is limited to seconds.

phase1_review.q: Calc: a limb muscle attaches 3 cm from the joint axis and holds a load whose line of force is 30 cm from the axis. What muscle force balances a 100 N load, and what is the mechanical advantage?
phase1_review.a: Torque balance: F_M × 3 = 100 × 30 → F_M = 1000 N. MA = 3/30 = 0.1 (must generate 10× the load force).

phase1_review.q: Which anaerobic-vs-aerobic sequencing rule should shape early weeks of a Hyrox block, per Ch 5-6?
phase1_review.a: Neural (anaerobic) and central (aerobic Q/SV) adaptations arrive first; peripheral/structural gains (mitochondria, capillaries, hypertrophy) accrue later — so prioritize drive, technique, and efficiency early, then build volume/capacity.

phase1_review.q: What sarcoplasm-vs-mitochondria distinction separates the anaerobic from the aerobic systems?
phase1_review.a: Phosphagen and glycolytic (anaerobic) reactions occur in the sarcoplasm without O2; Krebs cycle and electron transport (oxidative) occur in the mitochondria and require O2.

polarized_training.q: In a polarized model, is the ~80/20 split based on training minutes or session count?
polarized_training.a: Session count — the proportion of sessions that are easy vs hard, not the share of total minutes.

polarized_training.q: Name the three intensity zones of the 5E VT model relative to thresholds.
polarized_training.a: Z1 below LT1/VT1 (easy), Z2 between LT1 and LT2 (grey/threshold), Z3 above LT2/MLSS (hard).

polarized_training.q: Define the 'grey zone' and why polarized training avoids living there.
polarized_training.a: Z2 between LT1 and LT2 — too hard to allow recovery, too easy to drive top-end adaptation. Chronic grey-zone work blunts both ends (junk miles).

polarized_training.q: What approximate blood-lactate values bracket the grey zone (Z2) in the 5E model?
polarized_training.a: ~2 mmol/L (LT1, top of easy Z1) up to ~4 mmol/L (LT2/MLSS, bottom of hard Z3).

polarized_training.q: The 5E five-zone scale is Seiler/Norwegian-derived. What lactate range and session duration define its easiest zone (Z1)?
List four ways 5E says you can monitor/prescribe aerobic intensity when VO2max isn't measured live.
polarized_training.a: %HRR or %MHR (heart rate), RPE, blood lactate, and exercise velocity/power (METs in clinical populations).

polarized_training.q: An athlete does 10 sessions/week: 8 easy Z1, 1 threshold Z2, 1 VO2 Z3. Is this polarized, pyramidal, or threshold-biased?
polarized_training.a: Pyramidal-leaning: easy dominates (80%) but hard (Z3) is only 10% with a threshold session present. True polarized would drop the Z2 session and push ~20% into Z3, minimizing the middle.

polarized_training.q: Why does keeping easy days truly easy matter for the hard days?
polarized_training.a: Easy Z1 keeps autonomic/cardiac cost low so you recover; that lets the ~20% hard sessions be done at genuine Z3 quality. Grey-zone easy days accumulate fatigue and dull the hard days.

polarized_training.q: Compute the session-count polarization for a 4-week block: 48 easy, 4 grey, 8 hard sessions. What % is easy vs hard?
polarized_training.a: 60 sessions total → 48/60 = 80% easy, 8/60 = 13% hard, 4/60 = 7% grey. Roughly 80/20 with a small grey slice — near-polarized.

polarized_training.q: Why does 5E prefer the term 'extensive endurance' over 'long slow distance' (LSD)?
polarized_training.a: LSD implies just going slow for long; the real goal is extending aerobic capacity. Extensive endurance keeps the emphasis on the adaptation (mitochondria, fat use, lactate clearance), not merely slow pace.

polarized_training.q: What is the practical 'talk test' target for a Z1/extensive-endurance session?
polarized_training.a: Conversational — the athlete can talk without undue respiratory distress, a pace they feel they could sustain nearly indefinitely (below LT1).

polarized_training.q: How is the polarized model distinct from a 'threshold' or 'sweet-spot' distribution?
polarized_training.a: Threshold/sweet-spot concentrates volume in the grey Z2 band; polarized deliberately minimizes Z2 and splits work into easy Z1 and hard Z3 extremes.

lactate_thresholds_zones.q: What defines LT1 (lactate threshold) and what does it physiologically signal?
lactate_thresholds_zones.a: The exercise intensity where blood lactate first rises abruptly above baseline. It signals a significantly increased reliance on glycolysis and corresponds well with the ventilatory threshold.

lactate_thresholds_zones.q: At roughly what %VO2max does LT1 appear in untrained vs. trained athletes?
lactate_thresholds_zones.a: ~50-60% VO2max in untrained individuals, ~70-80% VO2max in aerobically trained athletes.

lactate_thresholds_zones.q: What blood-lactate concentration marks OBLA / LT2?
lactate_thresholds_zones.a: Approximately 4 mmol/L — the second inflection in the lactate-accumulation curve, termed onset of blood lactate accumulation (OBLA) or LT2.

The highest exercise intensity at which lactate production equals clearance, so blood lactate plateaus rather than climbing. It marks the heavy-to-severe transition and is synonymous with critical power/critical speed.

lactate_thresholds_zones.q: Why is MLSS often considered a better endurance predictor than VO2max?
lactate_thresholds_zones.a: It reflects the highest sustainable steady-state intensity an athlete can actually hold, whereas VO2max is a ceiling that many similarly-gifted athletes share; the winner is usually whoever sustains the highest %VO2max without accumulating lactate.

lactate_thresholds_zones.q: What happens to LT and OBLA with training, and by what mechanisms?
lactate_thresholds_zones.a: They shift rightward (occur at higher intensities), via increased mitochondrial content, reduced catecholamine release at high intensity, and greater reliance on free fatty acids — so the athlete holds a higher %VO2max without acidosis.

lactate_thresholds_zones.q: In a lactate-anchored zone model, what lactate values bracket the three intensity domains?
lactate_thresholds_zones.a: Zone 1 (aerobic) sits below ~2 mmol/L, threshold work is the 2-4 mmol/L band, and high-intensity/anaerobic zones are above ~4 mmol/L.

lactate_thresholds_zones.q: Why anchor training zones to measured LT1/LT2 rather than %HRmax tables?
lactate_thresholds_zones.a: Threshold heart rates vary widely between individuals and shift with fitness, heat, and fatigue, so generic %HRmax tables misplace zones for many athletes.

lactate_thresholds_zones.q: Why must zones be re-anchored as an athlete gets fitter?
lactate_thresholds_zones.a: As VO2max, LT, MLSS, and economy improve, a fixed absolute intensity becomes a lower relative stress, reducing the training stimulus — so intensity must be raised (thresholds re-tested) to keep progressing.

lactate_thresholds_zones.q: Why is training above MLSS (e.g., ~85% VO2max) limited to short durations?
lactate_thresholds_zones.a: Above MLSS, lactate production exceeds clearance so it accumulates continuously; the resulting acidosis drives fatigue, capping such efforts at roughly 20-30 minutes before failure.

lactate_thresholds_zones.q: A Hyrox athlete has LT1 HR 147 and LT2 HR 158. Where should a truly easy Z2 cap and a threshold session target sit?
lactate_thresholds_zones.a: Cap easy/Z2 below LT1 (~132-145) so it stays aerobic; hold threshold work in the LT1-LT2 band (~147-158) to raise MLSS. Sustained work above 158 becomes supra-MLSS and can only be held briefly.

lactate_thresholds_zones.q: How does improved exercise economy interact with LT and VO2max?
lactate_thresholds_zones.a: Better economy lowers the energy cost at a given velocity, which can raise effective VO2max utilization and lactate threshold — the athlete goes faster at the same metabolic cost.

zone2_mitochondria.q: Which master regulator drives mitochondrial biogenesis in response to endurance training?
Roughly how much ATP is produced from the complete oxidation of one glucose molecule?
zone2_mitochondria.a: ~32 ATP (about 34 if starting from muscle glycogen, since the first ATP-consuming glycolysis step is bypassed).

zone2_mitochondria.q: Why can fat oxidation supply so much more ATP than glucose oxidation per molecule?
zone2_mitochondria.a: Beta-oxidation of one triglyceride (three ~16-carbon fatty acids) yields >300 ATP vs ~32 from one glucose — fats have far greater ATP-synthesis capacity, just at a slower rate.

zone2_mitochondria.q: At rest, what fraction of ATP comes from fat vs carbohydrate, and how does that shift as intensity rises?
zone2_mitochondria.a: ~70% fat / ~30% CHO at rest; as intensity increases, substrate preference shifts toward carbohydrate, approaching ~100% CHO at high-intensity aerobic work.

zone2_mitochondria.q: Name the muscular/cellular adaptations to aerobic training that increase fat use.
zone2_mitochondria.a: ↑ mitochondrial size + number, ↑ myoglobin, ↑ oxidative (fat-metabolizing) enzymes, ↑ capillary density, and ↑ stored intramuscular triglyceride — muscle burns more fat and spares glycogen.

zone2_mitochondria.q: Why does Zone-2 training improve the pace/power you can hold BELOW threshold, not just easy-day comfort?
zone2_mitochondria.a: It raises mitochondrial density, capillarization, and MCT1 lactate-clearance capacity, so you oxidize more fat and clear lactate faster at any given absolute intensity — shifting the whole lactate curve right.

zone2_mitochondria.q: Where does Zone-2 sit relative to the lactate thresholds, and why that intensity?
zone2_mitochondria.a: Just below LT1 — high enough to maximally stimulate Type-I fat oxidation and PGC-1α, but low enough that blood lactate stays near baseline.

zone2_mitochondria.q: What blood lactate concentration approximately defines OBLA (LT2)?
At what %VO2max does LT1 typically occur in untrained vs aerobically trained people?
zone2_mitochondria.a: ~50-60% VO2max untrained; ~70-80% VO2max in trained athletes.

zone2_mitochondria.q: What does it mean that training 'shifts LT/OBLA to the right'?
zone2_mitochondria.a: Lactate begins accumulating at a higher exercise intensity/%VO2max, so the athlete sustains faster paces without metabolic acidosis — driven by ↑ mitochondrial content and reduced catecholamine response.

zone2_mitochondria.q: What is the rate-limiting enzyme of the Krebs cycle and how is it regulated?
zone2_mitochondria.a: Isocitrate dehydrogenase (isocitrate→α-ketoglutarate); stimulated by ADP and inhibited by ATP, matching cycle flux to energy demand.
The ability to efficiently switch between and oxidize fat and carbohydrate as fuel across different exercise intensities.

zone2_mitochondria.q: Why is Zone-2 benefit described as dose-dependent, and what does that imply for a Hyrox base block?
zone2_mitochondria.a: The biogenesis/mitochondrial signal scales with accumulated easy volume, not intensity — so a base block should be built on high Z2 volume rather than frequent threshold sessions.

concurrent_interference.q: How does 5E primarily explain the interference effect, before invoking molecular pathways?
concurrent_interference.a: As a fatigue/quality problem: high-intensity, high-volume, or high-frequency aerobic work degrades the quality of the resistance stimulus, blunting strength and power gains.

concurrent_interference.q: Which molecular pathways do endurance vs resistance training activate, and how do they conflict?
concurrent_interference.a: Endurance → AMPK (energy-sensing, mitochondrial/catabolic); resistance → Akt/mTOR (protein synthesis, hypertrophy). Elevated AMPK can suppress mTOR, attenuating the growth/strength response.

concurrent_interference.q: Is the interference effect symmetric? Explain.
concurrent_interference.a: No — asymmetric. High-intensity/volume aerobic training impairs strength and power, but heavy resistance training has little to no negative effect on aerobic power and can even improve economy.

concurrent_interference.q: Which is hit harder by concurrent training — max strength or power/RFD?
concurrent_interference.a: Power and rate of force development. Häkkinen found similar strength gains in concurrent vs RT-only groups, but only the RT-only group improved RFD.

concurrent_interference.q: Name the three aerobic-training variables that most increase interference magnitude.
concurrent_interference.a: Aerobic intensity, volume, and frequency. Low, easy-volume aerobic work causes little interference.

concurrent_interference.q: What did Kraemer's 5-group study reveal at the muscle-fiber level?
concurrent_interference.a: The combined group increased only type IIa fiber area, whereas the resistance-only group grew both type I and type II fibers — a cellular signature of the antagonism between the two stimuli.

concurrent_interference.q: How does increasing recovery between modes affect interference? Cite the Sale finding.
concurrent_interference.a: More separation reduces interference. Sale showed 4 days/week (2 RT + 2 aerobic, separated) beat 2 days/week combined for leg-press 1RM — 25% vs 13% gains.

concurrent_interference.q: Why do distance runners often ADD resistance training despite the interference effect?
concurrent_interference.a: Because heavy RT barely affects aerobic power and improves running economy and RFD, so it enhances endurance performance rather than harming it — the asymmetry works in their favor.

concurrent_interference.q: A Hyrox athlete does VO₂ intervals AM and heavy squats PM same day. Better arrangement?
concurrent_interference.a: Separate the hard modes onto different days, or put the priority quality (heavy squats, when strength/power is the goal) first and fresh, with the hard aerobic session hours later or on another day, to protect RFD.

concurrent_interference.q: Does sequence within concurrent training matter?
concurrent_interference.a: Yes — the order of resistance vs aerobic work can influence the magnitude of adaptation, so schedule the priority quality first while fresh.

concurrent_interference.q: Why is same-session concurrent training (3×/wk) often less damaging than 6 consecutive training days?
concurrent_interference.a: It builds in at least one rest day between sessions; the 6-consecutive-day model likely triggers overtraining/insufficient recovery, worsening the incompatibility.

endurance_determinants.q: Name the three primary determinants of endurance performance.
endurance_determinants.a: VO₂max (aerobic-power ceiling), fractional utilization / lactate threshold (% of VO₂max held before lactate accumulates), and movement economy (energy cost per pace or power).

endurance_determinants.q: Write the physiological equation for VO₂max and say which side dominates in trained non-elites.
endurance_determinants.a: VO₂max = maximal cardiac output × a-vO₂ difference. Mostly central — driven by max cardiac output (chiefly ↑stroke volume), since HRmax stays flat or drops slightly.

endurance_determinants.q: Why do well-trained athletes keep improving even after VO₂max plateaus?
endurance_determinants.a: Because gains shift to a higher lactate threshold (racing at a greater %VO₂max) and better economy — both raise performance without lifting the ceiling. Most VO₂max gain is done within 6-12 months.

endurance_determinants.q: Two runners both have VO₂max = 50 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹. Runner A's LT is at 80% VO₂max, Runner B's at 70%. What usable-power gap does that create?
endurance_determinants.a: A works at 0.80 × 50 = 40; B at 0.70 × 50 = 35. A can sustain 5 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ more — faster at threshold, all else (economy) equal.

endurance_determinants.q: Define MLSS and name its synonyms.
endurance_determinants.a: Maximal lactate steady state: the highest intensity where lactate production still equals clearance, marking the heavy-to-severe boundary. Synonymous with critical power (CP) and critical speed (CS).

endurance_determinants.q: Which single lab measure is often a better predictor of endurance result than VO₂max, and why?
endurance_determinants.a: Lactate threshold (LT) / MLSS — it captures the fraction of the aerobic ceiling an athlete can actually hold in a race, whereas VO₂max only sets the ceiling's height.

endurance_determinants.q: What is exercise economy, and how can two athletes with equal VO₂max and LT still differ in performance?
endurance_determinants.a: Economy is the O₂/energy cost of a given pace or power. The more economical athlete uses less O₂ at that pace, so they can race at a higher target pace for the same energy — winning despite identical VO₂max and LT.

endurance_determinants.q: An untrained beginner starts an aerobic program. Roughly how much can VO₂max rise, and over what window?
endurance_determinants.a: Roughly 5-30% depending on starting fitness and genetics (the 5E cites about 20% in previously untrained people), with most of the adaptation achieved inside a 6-12 month training period; after that, performance gains come mainly from economy and LT.

endurance_determinants.q: Physiologically, what raises the a-vO₂ difference with aerobic training?
endurance_determinants.a: Increased capillary density (shorter O₂ diffusion distance) plus more and larger mitochondria and higher myoglobin/oxidative-enzyme content — the muscle extracts more O₂ per unit blood.

endurance_determinants.q: Give the two training methods that best target economy vs. lactate threshold for a plateaued athlete.
endurance_determinants.a: Economy: HIIT with a technique focus (longer rests) plus heavy strength for stiffness/elastic return. LT/MLSS: threshold/pace-tempo work and shorter-rest high-intensity sessions that hold near MLSS.

endurance_determinants.q: Does aerobic training change HRmax? What actually drives the higher maximal cardiac output?
