endurance_determinants.a: HRmax is unchanged or drops slightly. The higher max cardiac output comes from a larger stroke volume — bigger left-ventricle chamber and stronger, more forceful contractions.

endurance_determinants.q: Why does improving economy indirectly help both VO₂max utilization and lactate threshold?
endurance_determinants.a: A more economical athlete uses less O₂ and produces less lactate at any given pace, so a fixed pace sits at a lower fraction of VO₂max and below threshold — effectively raising the usable ceiling and LT for that effort.

durability_decoupling.q: Why can VO₂max and lactate threshold, measured fresh, misjudge an athlete's real race capability?
durability_decoupling.a: Both are measured rested, but competition is decided after hours of fatigue. Durability — how those markers resist drift over time — is what actually holds pace late, and a fresh lab test says nothing about it.

durability_decoupling.q: Define aerobic decoupling and state what a value under ~5% suggests.
durability_decoupling.a: It's the drift in HR relative to pace/power across a steady effort. Under ~5% on a long session suggests a well-developed aerobic base; higher suggests an under-built base or that the pace exceeded true Zone 2.

durability_decoupling.q: Which 5E aerobic adaptations underpin durability the most?
durability_decoupling.a: ↑ mitochondrial + capillary density, ↑ oxidative enzymes, ↑ fat oxidation and glycogen sparing — they let the muscle keep meeting demand oxidatively and delay the fuel/metabolic drift that erodes late-race pace.

durability_decoupling.q: What single training input most develops durability?
durability_decoupling.a: Accumulated easy Zone-2 / aerobic volume plus long steady sessions — the same enzymatic and mitochondrial base 5E credits for endurance adaptation.

durability_decoupling.q: How does glycogen sparing relate to holding pace late in a long event?
durability_decoupling.a: Trained muscle burns proportionally more fat at a given intensity, using less glycogen. Delaying glycogen depletion pushes back the fuel-driven fade, so pace and HR stay coupled longer.

durability_decoupling.q: An athlete's easy run shows 9% decoupling. Give two plausible explanations and the fix for each.
durability_decoupling.a: (1) Under-built aerobic base → add Z2 volume and long steady sessions. (2) The 'easy' pace was actually above true Zone 2 → slow down / cap HR so the effort is genuinely aerobic.

durability_decoupling.q: After VO₂max plateaus (~6-12 months), what keeps endurance performance improving per 5E?
durability_decoupling.a: Continued gains in exercise economy and a higher lactate threshold — the athlete races at a higher %VO₂max for the same cost, even without a further VO₂max rise.

durability_decoupling.q: Two runners share VO₂max 50 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹; thresholds are 80% vs 70%. What absolute VO₂ gap can the first sustain, and why does it matter?
durability_decoupling.a: 80% of 50 = 40; 70% of 50 = 35; so the first sustains ~5 mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ more. All else equal (economy), that's a higher sustainable speed and superior performance.

durability_decoupling.q: A 3-hour ride starts at 250 W / 140 bpm (first half avg) and ends at 250 W / 154 bpm (second half). Estimate decoupling and interpret it.
durability_decoupling.a: Using HR:power ratio, first half 140/250 = 0.560, second 154/250 = 0.616; drift = (0.616−0.560)/0.560 ≈ 10%. Well above 5% → base gap or the effort exceeded true Zone 2.

durability_decoupling.q: Why are aerobic adaptations especially vulnerable to detraining, and what does that imply for a taper?
durability_decoupling.a: They rest on an enzymatic/mitochondrial basis that decays fast (VO₂max ↓4-14% in short-term detraining). A taper cuts volume/frequency but keeps intensity, so adaptations are preserved rather than lost.

durability_decoupling.q: How does capillary + mitochondrial density improvement translate into a lower decoupling reading?
durability_decoupling.a: More capillaries shorten O₂ diffusion distance and more mitochondria raise oxidative capacity, so a given pace stays comfortably aerobic — HR has less reason to drift upward over a long steady effort.

Aerobic base is the accumulated capacity (capillaries, mitochondria, enzymes, fat oxidation) built by easy volume; durability is the performance outcome — those capacities resisting decay over hours of work.

hiit_interval_design.q: What four variables define any interval prescription?
hiit_interval_design.a: Work duration, work intensity, recovery duration, and recovery intensity.

hiit_interval_design.q: What is the work duration and work:rest ratio for classic interval training?
hiit_interval_design.a: 3-5 min work (can be as short as 30 s) at intensities close to VO₂max, with rest equal to work → W:R ≈ 1:1.

hiit_interval_design.q: How is HIIT defined relative to metabolic thresholds?
hiit_interval_design.a: Repeated bouts above MLSS / critical VO₂ interspersed with brief recovery periods.

hiit_interval_design.q: According to Buchheit & Laursen, what session dose optimally stimulates VO₂max?
hiit_interval_design.a: Accumulating several total minutes above ~90% of VO₂max within the session.
20 s of work / 10 s of rest repeated 7-8 times to exhaustion.

hiit_interval_design.q: State an appropriate work:rest scheme for long-interval HIIT.
hiit_interval_design.a: About 2-3 min at or above 90% VO₂max with ~2 min of passive or low-intensity active recovery.

hiit_interval_design.q: Why prefer longer (2-4 min) supra-MLSS reps over all-out sprints for raising threshold/VO₂max?
hiit_interval_design.a: Longer supra-MLSS reps accumulate far more total time near VO₂max while staying repeatable; all-out sprints fatigue too fast to build that aerobic dose.

hiit_interval_design.q: How should the first rep of a well-paced interval session feel, and why?
hiit_interval_design.a: Almost too easy — even pacing lets you hit target intensity across the whole series instead of fading after a heroic first rep.

hiit_interval_design.q: What happens if HIIT recovery is too short? Too long?
hiit_interval_design.a: Too short → poor-quality subsequent reps and higher injury risk; too long → the glycolytic stimulus fades and you lose much of the benefit.

hiit_interval_design.q: Contrast the response to short (<45 s) vs long (2-4 min) HIIT intervals.
hiit_interval_design.a: Short/higher-intensity reps favor neuromuscular power and exercise economy; longer reps raise fast-glycolysis contribution and blood lactate, improving lactate metabolism and LT.

hiit_interval_design.q: What are the two ways to run pace/tempo (threshold) training, and at what intensity?
hiit_interval_design.a: At or slightly above MLSS: steady/continuous for ~20-30 min, or intermittent 'cruise/tempo intervals' at the same intensity with brief recoveries.

hiit_interval_design.q: A 5 min rep held at ~92% VO₂max — roughly how many such reps to bank ~15 min above 90% VO₂max in the session?
hiit_interval_design.a: About 3 reps (3 × 5 min = 15 min), assuming most of each rep sits above the 90% threshold once VO₂ rises.

hiit_interval_design.q: Why must interval/HIIT be built on an aerobic base and used sparingly?
hiit_interval_design.a: It is very stressful; without a firm aerobic-endurance base it drives excess fatigue and injury risk and can't be repeated at quality across the week.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: What is 'compromised running' and why does it dominate Hyrox outcomes?
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: Running on legs pre-fatigued and lactate-loaded from the prior station. With 8 km of running total, economy under that fatigue — not fresh speed — decides the overall result.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: How does MLSS explain the classic Hyrox pacing error?
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: MLSS is the heavy→severe boundary where lactate still steadies. Redlining early sleds pushes effort above MLSS, so lactate climbs continually and can't be cleared — the back-half runs detonate. Stay at/below MLSS.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: Which stations show the largest elite-to-average gap, and why physiologically?
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: Sled push and sled pull (plus grip-limited work). They spike effort above MLSS/critical power, so pacing errors there cost the most.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: Define MLSS and name its synonyms.
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: Maximal lactate steady state — the highest intensity where lactate production = clearance. Synonymous with critical power (CP) and critical speed (CS); marks the heavy→severe transition.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: What blood-lactate ranges typically bound the low, threshold, and high training zones?
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: Low/aerobic <2 mmol/L; threshold (LT) work 2-4 mmol/L; high/anaerobic >4 mmol/L.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: Which Ch 21 training type best builds the Hyrox 'engine at race pace,' and at what intensity/duration?
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: Pace/tempo (threshold) training — at or slightly above race pace, ≈ MLSS, ~20-30 min continuous (or cruise intervals). It raises LT, MLSS and economy.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: Why is even-to-negative pacing superior to fast-start pacing in Hyrox?
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: A fast start drives effort into the severe domain where lactate accumulates without clearing, degrading run economy for the remaining ~4-8 km. Even effort keeps you near MLSS and preserves back-half runs.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: Roughly what fraction of volume do elite endurance athletes run at easy (extensive-endurance) pace?
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: About 80% at extensive-endurance pace, with the remainder spent on tempo/interval/speed work — a polarized distribution.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: What does 'exercise economy' mean and why does it degrade across a Hyrox?
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: Economy is the energy/O2 cost of holding a given pace. Accumulating fatigue and lactate across stations raise that cost, so the same pace demands more late in the race.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: Interval training targets which intensity and work interval, and what is a typical W:R?
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: Close to VO2max, work intervals ~3-5 min (down to 30 s), with a 1:1 work-to-rest ratio for 3-5 min bouts.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: An athlete holds MLSS ≈ 4:20/km fresh but 4:45/km appears sustainable off the sled. What should pacing target dictate?
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: Pace to the compromised (off-station) sustainable effort — hold near the fatigued MLSS, ~4:45/km, rather than chasing the fresh figure and blowing up.

hyrox_demands_pacing.q: Why does a hot venue hurt a Hyrox athlete who paces by threshold effort?
hyrox_demands_pacing.a: Heat adds cardiovascular/thermoregulatory strain, raising HR and lactate at a given pace — effort crosses MLSS sooner, so the same splits become severe-domain and unsustainable.

sled_force_velocity.q: What type of force production does sled work primarily develop?
sled_force_velocity.a: Horizontal (propulsive) force via ground reaction, as opposed to the vertical force emphasized by squats and jumps.

sled_force_velocity.q: How does sled LOAD shift the training emphasis on the force–velocity curve?
sled_force_velocity.a: Heavy sleds bias the high-force/low-velocity end (acceleration mechanics, ground reaction); light sleds bias the high-velocity end (turnover, stride frequency).

sled_force_velocity.q: In the force–velocity curve (Fig 2.13), what happens to concentric torque as angular velocity increases?
sled_force_velocity.a: It declines — during shortening actions peak torque falls as contraction speed rises.

sled_force_velocity.q: Why can an athlete lower more weight than they can lift?
sled_force_velocity.a: Eccentric (lengthening) muscle actions produce greater torque than concentric; eccentric torque rises until ~90°/s before declining, so max force is eccentric.

sled_force_velocity.q: Why position the hands LOW on the prowler for a heavy push?
sled_force_velocity.a: Lower hands produce a more horizontal trunk and shin, increasing the ability to apply propulsive horizontal force and accelerate the sled.

sled_force_velocity.q: How should foot placement differ for breaking a heavy sled vs a lighter, sprint-like sled?
sled_force_velocity.a: Feet together to maximize horizontal force and overcome the heavy sled's inertia; a split stance for lighter loads to replicate a sprint start.

sled_force_velocity.q: Describe the stepping progression during the pushing phase.
sled_force_velocity.a: Start with short, relatively fast steps to apply horizontal force, then lengthen the strides and reduce ground-contact times as speed builds.

sled_force_velocity.q: During the push, where should the front foot stay relative to the hip, and why?
sled_force_velocity.a: Behind the hip — this optimizes the ability to apply propulsive horizontal force to the ground.

sled_force_velocity.q: What sled load has research used for heavy sled sprint training, and what improved?
sled_force_velocity.a: About 80% of body mass; it improved horizontal force production and 5 m sprint performance versus unresisted sprinting.

sled_force_velocity.q: What load range primed a PAPE (potentiation) sprint effect in the sled studies?
sled_force_velocity.a: Roughly 66–75% of body mass, enhancing subsequent sprint performance.

sled_force_velocity.q: Define the strength-to-mass ratio and its link to acceleration.
sled_force_velocity.a: Force capability relative to the mass being accelerated; a higher ratio directly improves the ability to accelerate the body or a sled.

sled_force_velocity.q: An athlete gains 15% body mass but only 10% more force. What happens to acceleration?
sled_force_velocity.a: The strength-to-mass ratio drops (1.10/1.15 < 1), so the ability to accelerate is reduced.

sled_force_velocity.q: For a 70 kg athlete, what heavy sled load would sit near the 80% body-mass benchmark?
sled_force_velocity.a: About 56 kg of external load (0.80 × 70), plus the sled's own mass — biasing the high-force end.

sled_force_velocity.q: Why does eccentric torque not rise indefinitely with velocity?
sled_force_velocity.a: It increases only until about 90°/s (1.57 rad/s), after which maximal torque capability declines gradually.

hybrid_periodization.q: Name the three planning models in 5E Ch 22 and which one best fits a hybrid athlete.
hybrid_periodization.a: Parallel (concurrent), sequential, and emphasis. The emphasis model fits hybrids best — it develops several qualities at once but rotates the focus, so you lean toward the limiting quality while maintaining the other.

hybrid_periodization.q: Why is the parallel/concurrent model poorly suited to advanced athletes?
hybrid_periodization.a: Because to keep stimulating adaptation the total training load must continually rise, and it eventually exceeds the advanced athlete's training tolerance. Novices and developmental athletes tolerate it fine.

hybrid_periodization.q: In the emphasis model, what happens to the three tiers of qualities in a given block?
hybrid_periodization.a: Some qualities are trained hard enough to drive adaptation, some are held at a maintenance dose, and some get only a minimal dose and are allowed to detrain slightly. Focus rotates ~every 2 weeks.

hybrid_periodization.q: State the fitness–fatigue paradigm and its key rate fact.
hybrid_periodization.a: Preparedness = fitness minus fatigue; every bout adds both. Fatigue dissipates ~two-thirds faster than fitness, so reducing load (taper/unload) drops fatigue quickly and lets the retained fitness surface as elevated preparedness.

hybrid_periodization.q: Apply fitness–fatigue: you feel flat mid-block despite good numbers earlier. What does the paradigm say and what do you do?
hybrid_periodization.a: High recent load has raised fitness but stacked more fatigue, so summed preparedness is depressed. Insert an unload/light microcycle — fatigue clears faster than fitness, so preparedness rebounds without losing the gains.

hybrid_periodization.q: What is DUP and how does it differ from the traditional (mislabeled 'linear') model?
hybrid_periodization.a: DUP swings load/volume/focus within the microcycle (e.g., a 6RM day, a 10RM day, a 3RM day). The traditional model keeps reps fixed and varies load day to day; it is still nonlinear because volume-load undulates, so calling it 'linear' is a misnomer.

hybrid_periodization.q: Why is 'linear periodization' considered a false label in 5E?
hybrid_periodization.a: A core tenet of periodization is removing linearity. Even the traditional model has nonlinear volume/intensity swings at the micro- and mesocycle level (volume-load changes even when reps are held), so it was never truly linear.

hybrid_periodization.q: In a concurrent week, why give the limiting quality's hard session the freshest slot?
hybrid_periodization.a: Fatigue and the interference effect degrade whatever quality you train second. Placing the priority hard work when you are freshest maximizes its adaptive stimulus.

hybrid_periodization.q: In hybrid periodization, what do you periodize at the block level and what do you hold roughly constant?
hybrid_periodization.a: Periodize training volume and quality emphasis across blocks; hold the polarized intensity distribution (~80/20) roughly constant rather than periodizing it.

hybrid_periodization.q: Give typical durations for a mesocycle and a microcycle.
hybrid_periodization.a: Mesocycle: 2–6 weeks, 4 weeks most common. Microcycle: several days to 2 weeks, 1 week (7 days) most common.

hybrid_periodization.q: You have a heavy-lift priority block but must maintain your run engine. Which model, and how do you set the dose tiers?
hybrid_periodization.a: Emphasis model: strength gets the adaptation-driving dose, aerobic work drops to a maintenance dose (enough intensity/frequency to hold it), and any non-priority quality can take a minimal dose. Rotate emphasis to the run side in the following block.

hybrid_periodization.q: A sample DUP week runs 4×6RM Tue, 3×10RM Thu, 5×3RM Sat. What focus does each day target?
hybrid_periodization.a: 6RM = strength focus, 10RM = hypertrophy focus, 3RM = power focus — load, volume, and training focus all vary within the microcycle.

heat_thermoregulation.q: Why do HR-based zones mislead during hot-weather training?
heat_thermoregulation.a: Heat diverts blood to skin for cooling, lowering stroke volume and raising HR at any given pace, so the same HR reflects a slower, lower-output effort — cap effort, don't chase pace.

heat_thermoregulation.q: List four chronic adaptations of heat acclimatization.
heat_thermoregulation.a: Earlier onset and higher sweat rate, lower sweat-onset and core/skin temperatures, expanded plasma (blood) volume, and reduced resting/exercise HR.

heat_thermoregulation.q: How fast do heat adaptations appear and decay?
heat_thermoregulation.a: ~75% within the first 4-6 days (fuller by ~10-14 d), but they decay ~2.5% per day once heat exposure stops and are largely lost within about a month.

heat_thermoregulation.q: At what fluid deficit does performance meaningfully decline?
heat_thermoregulation.a: About 2% of body mass; even 2-3% raises core temp and reduces stroke volume, cardiac output, power, strength and endurance.

heat_thermoregulation.q: An 80 kg athlete finishes 2.4 kg lighter after a hot session with <12 h to next bout. How much fluid should be replaced?
heat_thermoregulation.a: ~1.5 L per kg lost → 2.4 × 1.5 ≈ 3.6 L, with sufficient electrolytes to offset the extra urine output.

heat_thermoregulation.q: Weigh-in 82.0 kg, weigh-out 80.5 kg, drank 0.8 L, no urine, over 1 h. Sweat rate?
heat_thermoregulation.a: (82.0 − 80.5) kg = 1.5 L equivalent + 0.8 L drunk = 2.3 L lost in 1 h → ~2.3 L/h.

heat_thermoregulation.q: Why can plain-water over-drinking during long sweaty efforts be dangerous?
heat_thermoregulation.a: It dilutes blood sodium below ~130 mmol/L (hyponatremia), causing intracellular swelling; below ~120 mmol/L risks seizures, cerebral edema and death.

heat_thermoregulation.q: Which electrolytes are lost most in sweat, and which matters most to replace?
heat_thermoregulation.a: Mainly sodium and chloride, then potassium, magnesium, calcium; sodium is key because it helps retain ingested fluid and losses vary hugely (0.2 to >12.5 g/L).

heat_thermoregulation.q: How does heat acclimation differ from altitude training in its plasma-volume effect?
heat_thermoregulation.a: Heat acclimation expands plasma volume (↑stroke volume); altitude acutely reduces plasma volume, relying instead on later ↑red-cell production/hematocrit.

heat_thermoregulation.q: Why does a hot quality session cost more recovery than the intervals alone suggest?
heat_thermoregulation.a: Thermal strain and hypohydration add cardiovascular and recovery burden on top of the metabolic work, so total cost exceeds the same intervals done cool.

heat_thermoregulation.q: Why should heat-race prep keep some heat exposure right up to the event?
heat_thermoregulation.a: Adaptations are transient and decay ~2.5%/day; maintaining exposure and training preserves the plasma-volume and sweat gains until competition.

heat_thermoregulation.q: What sodium/carb concentration does the IOM recommend for sport drinks in prolonged hot-weather exercise?
heat_thermoregulation.a: ~20-30 mEq (460-690 mg) sodium per liter, 2-5 mEq potassium per liter, and 5-10% carbohydrate, served cool (10-15°C).

recovery_hrv_sleep.q: Distinguish overtraining the process from the overtraining syndrome (OTS).
recovery_hrv_sleep.a: OT is the process — chronic training/non-training stress beyond recovery capacity. OTS is the result: a long-term performance decrement (restore in weeks–months) with physiological/psychological maladaptation. Impaired performance is required for OTS to exist.

recovery_hrv_sleep.q: How does functional overreaching (FOR) differ from nonfunctional (NFOR)?
recovery_hrv_sleep.a: FOR is planned short-term overload; after brief rest it super-compensates → performance rises. NFOR accumulates more fatigue, takes longer to recover, and only returns to baseline — no benefit, athlete needlessly stressed.

recovery_hrv_sleep.q: Which performance quality is most sensitive to overtraining, and which is most protected?
recovery_hrv_sleep.a: Speed (sprinting/velocity) drops first, then power; maximal strength is one of the last to fall — strength is well-protected.

recovery_hrv_sleep.q: What is allostatic load and why does it matter for programming?
recovery_hrv_sleep.a: The cumulative 'wear and tear' from ALL stressors — training plus off-field factors (sleep, job, travel, nutrition, illness). It sums with training stress, so you must account for life stress or the same session can tip an athlete into OTS.

SOTS (sympathetic) = ↑epinephrine response, eventual β2-receptor down-regulation and impaired contractility; seen with strength/power. POTS (parasympathetic) = ↓resting HR and ↓epinephrine ('adrenal exhaustion'); seen in high-volume endurance athletes.

recovery_hrv_sleep.q: A masters runner's resting HR keeps dropping — is that good adaptation or a warning sign?
recovery_hrv_sleep.a: It depends. In most trainees ↓RHR = beneficial cardiovascular adaptation, but in an already highly trained endurance athlete with huge volume and falling performance it can signal parasympathetic OTS (POTS). Read it alongside performance and HRV trends, not in isolation.

recovery_hrv_sleep.q: Does every drop in performance mean overtraining?
recovery_hrv_sleep.a: No. Underperformance has many causes — mistimed peak/taper, undertraining, poor contest strategy, illness, injury, RED-S, motivation. OT is only one type; check timing and off-field factors before blaming excess training.

recovery_hrv_sleep.q: Describe the three-category monitoring escalation and its logic.
recovery_hrv_sleep.a: Cat I = daily, cheap, fast, convenient (e.g., RHR, HRV, RPE, short questionnaires). Cat II = every few days, more involved. Cat III = occasional, intrusive, expensive (e.g., bloods). Run Cat I first; escalate only when a level flags concern — avoids unnecessary testing.

recovery_hrv_sleep.q: What is the mandatory first step once OTS has developed, and typical recovery times?
recovery_hrv_sleep.a: Immediately cut training volume, intensity, or both (up to full cessation). Overreaching can recover in ~1 week; severe resistance-exercise OT up to ~4 weeks; high-volume endurance OT months to a year.

recovery_hrv_sleep.q: Which recovery modalities have the strongest supporting evidence per Table 24.4?
recovery_hrv_sleep.a: Modifying the training program (decrease volume/frequency/intensity) is most strongly supported, followed by quality sleep and training taper. Many modalities (massage, cryotherapy, supplements) are only 'possible/promising/inconclusive.'

recovery_hrv_sleep.q: Where do HRV and sleep fit into the 5E monitoring frame?
recovery_hrv_sleep.a: HRV and resting HR are listed cardiovascular measures in the athlete-monitoring program (Category-I daily markers); sleep records are a monitored input and quality sleep is a strongly-supported recovery strategy. Modern practice reads the multi-week HRV trend for readiness-guided load.

recovery_hrv_sleep.q: Why act on the HRV trend rather than a single morning reading?
recovery_hrv_sleep.a: A one-day dip is noisy (sleep timing, stress, alcohol, measurement error). A rising multi-week trend tracks positive adaptation; a sustained suppression flags incomplete recovery — that's the signal worth easing load for.
